docs.title: SNMP Service
docs.text:
LibreNMS network monitoring using official librenms/librenms Docker image. Provides SNMP polling, trap reception, device discovery, and alerting.

Official LibreNMS Documentation

For in-depth details beyond this Testbed as Code reference, see the official LibreNMS docs:

Topic URL Alerting Introduction https://docs.librenms.org/Alerting/ Alert Rules (syntax, options, examples) https://docs.librenms.org/Alerting/Rules/ Alert Templates https://docs.librenms.org/Alerting/Templates/ Alert Transports (mail, Slack, Microsoft Teams, Cisco Webex Teams…) https://docs.librenms.org/Alerting/Transports/ Alert Macros https://docs.librenms.org/Alerting/Macros/ Alert Testing https://docs.librenms.org/Alerting/Testing/ Fast Ping Checking https://docs.librenms.org/Extensions/Fast-Ping-Check/ SNMP Trap Handler https://docs.librenms.org/Extensions/SNMP-Trap-Handler/ Dispatcher Service https://docs.librenms.org/Extensions/Dispatcher-Service/ Docker Installation https://docs.librenms.org/Installation/Docker/ Configuration https://docs.librenms.org/Support/Configuration/ API Reference https://docs.librenms.org/API/ Alert Rules Collection (community rules) https://docs.librenms.org/Alerting/Rules/#alert-rules-collection

Multi-container architecture

LibreNMS uses multiple containers: main app, database (MariaDB), cache (Redis), dispatcher, and snmptrapd. All orchestrated via Docker Compose.

Configuration approach

Uses three-tier config: Docker environment (TZ, PUID/PGID), LibreNMS environment variables (librenms.env), and application config (librenms-config.yaml).

docs.title: Supported Images
docs.text: Image Supported Versions Notes librenms/librenms 26.2.0 (default) Official LibreNMS with all features mariadb 10.11 (default) Database backend redis 7.2-alpine (default) Cache and session storage

docs.title: Basic SNMP Configuration
docs.text:
network_services:
  snmp:
    image_name: "librenms/librenms"
    image_version: "26.2.0"
    db_password: "secure_password_here"
    db_root_password: "secure_root_password"
    web_password: "admin_password_here"
    snmp_community: "private"
    monitored_devices:
      - hostname: "192.168.1.1"
        community: "private"
        snmp_version: "v2c"

docs.title: Complete SNMP Example
docs.text:
network_services:
  snmp:
    service_name: "tbac_librenms"
    image_name: "librenms/librenms"
    image_version: "26.2.0"
    db_image_version: "10.11"
    redis_image: "redis:7.2-alpine"
    db_password: "YourStrongPassword123!"
    db_root_password: "YourRootPassword456!"
    web_user: "admin"
    web_password: "YourAdminPassword789!"
    timezone: "America/New_York"
    poller_threads: 16
    discovery_threads: 16
    poller_frequency: 300
    snmp_community: "private"
    snmp_version: "v2c"
    web_port: 8000
    trap_port: 162
    fast_ping: true
    fast_ping_interval: 60
    alert_default_only: false            # false = route alerts per rule's transports list
    alert_transports:
      - name: "Email Ops Team"
        type: "mail"
        is_default: true
        config:
          email: "ops@example.com"
          email_backend: smtp
          email_smtp_host: smtp.example.com
          email_smtp_port: 25
          email_from: '"LibreNMS" <librenms@example.com>'
      - name: "Slack Alerts"
        type: "slack"
        config:
          url: "https://hooks.slack.com/services/YOUR/WEBHOOK/URL"
    alert_rules:
      - name: "Port Down"
        severity: critical
        conditions:
          - field: ports.ifOperStatus
            operator: "="
            value: "down"
          - field: ports.ifAdminStatus
            operator: "="
            value: "up"
        transports: ["Email Ops Team", "Slack Alerts"]
      - name: "Device Down"
        severity: critical
        conditions:
          - field: devices.status
            operator: "="
            value: "0"
        transports: ["Email Ops Team"]
    monitored_devices:
      - hostname: "192.168.1.1"
        community: "private"
        snmp_version: "v2c"
        display_name: "Core Router"
      - hostname: "192.168.1.2"
        snmp_version: "v3"
        snmpv3_authname: "snmpuser"
        snmpv3_authpass: "authpassword123"
        snmpv3_authalgo: "SHA"
        snmpv3_cryptopass: "privacypassword456"
        snmpv3_cryptoalgo: "AES"
        # Note: snmpv3_authlevel is auto-detected (authPriv in this case)
        # based on which credentials are provided - no need to specify it

docs.title: SNMP Field Reference
docs.text:
Schema-required fields with defaults

Many fields marked Yes in the schema are provided automatically by defaults/defaults.tbac.yaml. If you use the defaults file, you only need to specify the three password fields (db_password, db_root_password, web_password) and any environment-specific overrides.

Field Type Default Required Description service_name string tbac_librenms No Service/directory name. Affects container names (<service_name>, <service_name>_db, <service_name>_dispatcher) and host directory path (<docker_compose_dir>/<service_name>/) image_name string librenms/librenms Yes Docker image for LibreNMS image_version string 26.2.0 Yes LibreNMS version tag db_image_version string 10.11 Yes MariaDB image version (e.g. 10.11) redis_image string redis:7.2-alpine Yes Redis image (e.g. redis:7.2-alpine) db_password string - Yes Database password — must be set; no default. Use ansible-vault db_root_password string - Yes MySQL root password — must be set; no default. Use ansible-vault web_password string - Yes Web UI admin password — must be set; no default. Use ansible-vault db_host string db Yes Database container name/hostname db_port integer 3306 Yes Database port (1-65535) db_name string librenms Yes Database name db_user string librenms Yes Database username web_user string admin Yes Web UI admin username timezone string UTC Yes Server timezone (e.g., America/New_York) poller_threads integer 16 Yes Number of parallel poller workers (1-64) discovery_threads integer 16 Yes Number of parallel discovery workers (1-64) poller_frequency integer 300 Yes Polling and alert evaluation interval in seconds (60-300). Lower values = faster alerts but more CPU/SNMP load snmp_community string private Yes Default SNMP v1/v2c community string snmp_version string v2c Yes Default SNMP version (v1, v2c, v3) snmp_timeout integer 5 Yes SNMP timeout in seconds (min: 1) snmp_retries integer 3 Yes SNMP retry attempts (min: 0) auto_discovery boolean true Yes Enable XDP-based auto-discovery web_port integer 8000 Yes Web interface HTTP port (1-65535) trap_port integer 162 Yes SNMP trap receiver UDP port (1-65535) fast_ping boolean true Yes Enable fast ICMP ping checks for quicker device down/up detection. Testbed as Code automatically configures this in the dispatcher. See Fast Ping Checking fast_ping_interval integer 300 Yes Fast ping interval in seconds (15-300). Lower values = faster detection but higher load. See Fast Ping Checking base_url string (auto-generated) No Custom base URL (e.g. https://librenms.example.com). Auto-generates http://<vm-ip>:<web_port> if omitted. Required for CSRF protection when using a reverse proxy enable_syslog boolean false No Enable syslog-ng receiver sidecar syslog_port integer 514 No Syslog UDP port (if enabled) alert_default_only boolean true No When true, alerts are sent only via transports with is_default: true. When false, alerts use per-rule transport lists. Does NOT disable alerting — to stop notifications, remove alert_transports. alert_transports list [] No Alert delivery methods (mail, slack, microsoft_teams, cisco_webex_teams, webhook) alert_rules list [] No Alert rules that trigger notifications (see Alert Rule Fields) monitored_devices list [] No List of devices to monitor via SNMP

docs.title: Mail Transport Config Fields
docs.text:
These fields go inside the config: block of a type: mail transport. They map directly to LibreNMS Mail Transport settings.

Data Config Key LibreNMS DB Key Type Default Required Description mail-contact mail-contact string email No Contact type: email (specified address), sysContact (from device SNMP), owners (device owners), role (LibreNMS user role) email email string - When mail-contact: email Recipient email address role role string "" When mail-contact: role LibreNMS user role name to send alerts to bcc bcc boolean false No Send as BCC instead of TO attach-graph attach-graph boolean true No Embed graph images in alert emails (requires @signedGraphTag in template) email_backend - string mail No Email backend: smtp, sendmail, or mail email_smtp_host - string localhost When smtp SMTP relay server hostname email_smtp_port - integer 25 No SMTP server port email_smtp_auth - boolean false No Enable SMTP authentication email_smtp_username - string - When auth SMTP username (use ansible-vault) email_smtp_password - string - When auth SMTP password (use ansible-vault) email_smtp_secure - string "" No SMTP encryption: tls, ssl, or "" (none) email_from - string LibreNMS@<container> No Sender "From" address

Transport config vs global email config

The first five fields (mail-contact, email, role, bcc, attach-graph) are per-transport settings stored in the alert_transports table. The email_* fields are global LibreNMS email settings configured via lnms config:set — Testbed as Code sets these in librenms-config.yaml automatically.

network_services:
  snmp:
    fast_ping: true
    alert_default_only: false
    alert_transports:
      - name: "Email Ops Team"
        type: "mail"
        is_default: true
        config:
          mail-contact: email           # Contact type: email, sysContact, owners, role
          email: "ops@example.com"      # Recipient address (when mail-contact: email)
          # role: "admin"              # LibreNMS role name (when mail-contact: role)
          bcc: false                    # Send as BCC instead of TO
          attach-graph: true            # Embed graphs in alert email
          # SMTP relay for alert emails (global settings)
          email_backend: smtp
          email_smtp_host: smtp.example.com
          email_smtp_port: 25
          email_from: '"My-LibreNMS" <librenms@example.com>'
          # For authenticated SMTP:
          # email_smtp_auth: true
          # email_smtp_username: "user@example.com"
          # email_smtp_password: "secret"
          # email_smtp_secure: tls

docs.title: Alert Transport Types
docs.text:
Supported alert transport types:

Type Description Required Fields mail Email alerts email, email_backend, email_smtp_host (when smtp) slack Slack webhook url, channel (optional) microsoft_teams Microsoft Teams webhook url, use-json (optional) cisco_webex_teams Cisco Webex Teams api-token, room-id, use-markdown (optional) webhook Generic HTTP webhook url, method (optional)

All transport types

LibreNMS supports 40+ transport types. Testbed as Code automates the five above. For the full list (PagerDuty, Telegram, Discord, etc.) see Alert Transports.

docs.title: Alert Transport Fields
docs.text:
Field Type Default Required Description name string - Yes Display name for the transport (must be unique). Referenced by alert_rules[].transports[] type string - Yes Transport type: mail, slack, microsoft_teams, cisco_webex_teams, webhook is_default boolean false No When true, this transport is automatically applied to all alert rules — even rules that don't explicitly list it in transports[]. Set this on your primary notification channel so no alerts are missed config map - Yes Type-specific configuration (see transport type table above for required keys)

Default transport behavior

Setting is_default: true on a transport means LibreNMS will use it for every alert rule, regardless of whether the rule's transports list includes it. This is useful for ensuring a primary email or chat channel always receives all alerts. You can still add additional non-default transports to specific rules via the transports field.

docs.title: Cisco Webex Teams Transport Config Fields
docs.text:
These fields go inside the config: block of a type: cisco_webex_teams transport. They map directly to LibreNMS Cisco Spark Transport settings.

Data Config Key LibreNMS DB Key Type Default Required Description api-token api-token string - Yes Webex Bot API token. Create at developer.webex.com/my-apps → Create a Bot room-id room-id string - Yes Webex space (room) ID. Get via GET https://webexapis.com/v1/rooms use-markdown use-markdown boolean false No Send alert messages using Markdown formatting 
alert_transports:
  - name: "Cisco Webex Teams Alerts"
    type: "cisco_webex_teams"
    is_default: true
    config:
      api-token: "NjE2YzY5..."          # Bot access token
      room-id: "Y2lzY29zcGFyay..."      # Room ID from API
      use-markdown: false                # Use Markdown formatting

Bot must be a space member

The bot must be added as a member of the Webex space to post messages. Search the bot's username in the space's People panel to add it.

docs.title: Slack Transport Config Fields
docs.text:
These fields go inside the config: block of a type: slack transport. They map to LibreNMS Slack Transport settings.

Data Config Key LibreNMS DB Key Type Default Required Description url slack-url string - Yes Slack Incoming Webhook URL channel slack-channel string "" No Override channel (e.g. #alerts). Leave empty to use webhook default

Slack Author

Testbed as Code automatically sets the slack-author field to "LibreNMS" in the database. This is not configurable.

alert_transports:
  - name: "Slack Alerts"
    type: "slack"
    config:
      url: "https://hooks.slack.com/services/T00000/B00000/XXXXXXX"
      channel: "#network-alerts"       # Optional: override webhook default channel

docs.title: Microsoft Teams Transport Config Fields
docs.text:
These fields go inside the config: block of a type: microsoft_teams transport. They map to LibreNMS Microsoft Teams Transport settings.

Data Config Key LibreNMS DB Key Type Default Required Description url msteam-url string - Yes Microsoft Teams Incoming Webhook URL use-json use-json boolean false No Send alert as JSON MessageCard instead of Markdown. Your alert template must be valid MessageCard JSON 
alert_transports:
  - name: "Teams NOC Channel"
    type: "microsoft_teams"
    config:
      url: "https://outlook.office.com/webhook/YOUR-WEBHOOK-URL"
      use-json: false                  # Set true for JSON MessageCard format

Creating a Teams webhook

In your Teams channel, go to Connectors → Incoming Webhook → Configure. Copy the generated URL and use it as the url value.

docs.title: Webhook (API) Transport Config Fields
docs.text:
These fields go inside the config: block of a type: webhook transport. They map to LibreNMS API Transport settings. Use this for generic HTTP integrations (PagerDuty, custom endpoints, etc.).

Data Config Key LibreNMS DB Key Type Default Required Description url api-url string - Yes Webhook endpoint URL method api-method string POST No HTTP method: GET, POST, or PUT 
alert_transports:
  - name: "PagerDuty Webhook"
    type: "webhook"
    config:
      url: "https://events.pagerduty.com/generic/2010-04-15/create_event.json"
      method: "POST"

docs.title: Transport Type → LibreNMS Internal Mapping
docs.text:
Testbed as Code uses user-friendly type names that map to LibreNMS internal transport types:

Testbed as Code type Value LibreNMS Internal Type LibreNMS PHP Class DB Config Keys mail mail Mail.php mail-contact, email, role, bcc, attach-graph slack slack Slack.php slack-url, slack-channel, slack-author microsoft_teams msteams Msteams.php msteam-url, use-json cisco_webex_teams ciscospark Ciscospark.php api-token, room-id, use-markdown webhook api Api.php api-url, api-method

Hyphen vs underscore in config keys

Config keys with hyphens (e.g. mail-contact, api-token, room-id) can also be written with underscores (e.g. mail_contact, api_token, room_id). Both forms are accepted in your data YAML. Testbed as Code templates handle both transparently.

docs.title: Alert Rules
docs.text:
Alert rules define when to fire notifications. Each rule has conditions evaluated against LibreNMS database fields, and a list of transports to notify. Rules are transport-agnostic — the same rule can notify email, Cisco Webex Teams, Microsoft Teams, and Slack simultaneously.

Official reference

See Alert Rules — Options for the full list of rule options, and Alert Rules — Examples for community examples.

alert_rules:
  - name: "Port Down"
    severity: critical
    conditions:
      - field: ports.ifOperStatus
        operator: "="
        value: "down"
      - field: ports.ifAdminStatus
        operator: "="
        value: "up"
    transports: ["Email Ops Team", "Cisco Webex Teams NOC"]

docs.title: Alert Rule Fields
docs.text:
Field Type Default Required Description name string - Yes Unique rule name severity string critical No critical, warning, or ok condition string AND No Logical operator: AND or OR conditions list - Yes List of match conditions (see below) transports list [] No Transport names to notify (must match alert_transports[].name). Transports marked is_default: true are automatically added to all rules count integer 1 No Max alerts per event before stopping. 1 = alert once then wait for recovery (recommended). -1 = unlimited re-alerts every interval until condition clears delay string 0m No Delay before first alert (e.g. 5m). Useful to avoid alerts for transient issues interval string 0m No Re-alert interval until max count is reached (e.g. 5m). Use 0m when count: 1 (no re-alerting) mute boolean false No Mute transport notifications (alert still visible in Web UI) invert boolean false No Invert rule match — alert when condition is not met instead of when it is recovery boolean true No Send recovery notification when condition clears

One-time alerting (recommended)

Use count: 1 with interval: 0m to receive one email when a device/port goes down and one recovery email when it comes back up. Setting count: -1 causes continuous re-alerts every interval — avoid this unless you explicitly want repeated notifications.

See LibreNMS Alert Rules — Options for official documentation on these fields.

docs.title: Condition Fields
docs.text:
Field Type Required Description field string Yes Database field (e.g. ports.ifOperStatus, devices.status) operator string Yes Comparison: =, !=, >, <, >=, <=, like, not like, regex, starts, ends, in, not in, between, is null, is not null value string Yes Value to match against

Available entities and operators

The field uses the format table.column from the LibreNMS database. Common tables: devices, ports, processors, mempools, storage, syslog. See Alerting Rules — Syntax for all supported operators and Alerting Entities for available fields.

Docker interface auto-exclusion

When any condition uses ports.ifOperStatus, Testbed as Code automatically appends exclusions for Docker virtual interfaces (docker0, br-*, veth*) to prevent false Port Down alerts. No data-file changes needed.

docs.title: Common Alert Rules
docs.text: Rule Conditions Description Port Down ports.ifOperStatus = down AND ports.ifAdminStatus = up Admin-enabled port went operationally down Device Down devices.status = 0 Device is unreachable High CPU processors.processor_usage > 80 CPU utilization above 80% High Memory mempools.mempool_perc > 90 Memory utilization above 90% Storage Full storage.storage_perc > 95 Disk usage above 95%

docs.title: Monitored Device Fields
docs.text: Field Type Default Required Description hostname string - Yes Device hostname or IP address community string (inherits) For v1/v2c SNMP community string (default: inherits from snmp_community) snmp_version string (inherits) No SNMP version: v1, v2c, v3 (default: inherits from snmp_version) snmp_port integer 161 No SNMP port (1-65535) display_name string - No Custom display name in web UI snmpv3_authlevel string - No SNMPv3 security level: noAuthNoPriv, authNoPriv, or authPriv. Optional — auto-inferred by Rule 303 from which credentials are present. Only specify to override inference snmpv3_authname string - For v3 auth SNMPv3 username (security name) snmpv3_authpass string - For v3 auth Authentication password (use ansible-vault) snmpv3_authalgo string - For v3 auth Auth algorithm: MD5, SHA, SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, or SHA-512 (default: SHA; MD5 is deprecated) snmpv3_cryptopass string - For authPriv Privacy/encryption password (use ansible-vault) snmpv3_cryptoalgo string - For authPriv Privacy algorithm: DES or AES

docs.title: Device Addition Process
docs.text:
When Testbed as Code adds monitored devices to LibreNMS, it automatically performs a three-step process for each device:

  1. Device Addition: Adds the device to LibreNMS with the specified SNMP credentials
  2. Initial Discovery: Runs LibreNMS discovery to find ports, CPUs, storage, sensors, and OS type. Without discovery, the ports table remains empty and SNMP traps (linkDown/linkUp) cannot be matched to interfaces
  3. Initial Poll: Collects metrics for everything discovery found. Must run after discovery so polling has interfaces/sensors to collect data for

This ensures devices are immediately operational with complete monitoring data. The entire process runs automatically during deployment.

Manual device operations

After deployment, you can manually trigger discovery and polling: 

# SSH to the LibreNMS VM
docker exec -u librenms tbac_librenms lnms device:discover <hostname>
docker exec -u librenms tbac_librenms lnms device:poll <hostname>

docs.title: Accessing LibreNMS Web Interface
docs.text:
After deployment, Testbed as Code displays the LibreNMS access information:

------------------------------------------------------------
  LibreNMS SNMP Service Deployed Successfully
------------------------------------------------------------
  Container: tbac_librenms
  Web Interface: http://<vm-ip>:8000
  Web User: admin
  Database: db:3306
  SNMP Community: private
  SNMP Version: v2c
  Monitored Devices: 2
------------------------------------------------------------

Login to the web interface at http://<vm-ip>:8000 with the configured web_user and web_password.

First login

The web interface is ready immediately after deployment. All configured devices will already be added and have completed initial discovery and polling.

docs.title: Container Architecture
docs.text:
LibreNMS deployment consists of multiple containers:

Container Name Purpose Ports <service_name> Main LibreNMS web interface and core services <web_port>:8000 (default: 8000) <service_name>_db MariaDB database for LibreNMS data Internal only <service_name>_redis Redis cache for sessions and queues Internal only <service_name>_dispatcher Polling and discovery orchestration Internal only <service_name>_snmptrapd SNMP trap receiver <trap_port>:162 (TCP/UDP, default: 162) <service_name>_syslog Syslog-ng receiver (optional) <syslog_port>:514 (TCP/UDP, if enabled)

Automatic configuration

Testbed as Code automatically: - Creates the admin user with specified credentials - Configures fast ping checks in the dispatcher - Sets up alert transports and rules - Adds monitored devices with discovery and initial poll - Configures SMTP settings from mail transport config

docs.title: Validation Rules
docs.text: Rule Description 302 Docker image validation — librenms/librenms is validated against the whitelist with version pinning 303 SNMP/LibreNMS service configuration validation — credential strength (blocks common weak passwords), cisco-password restriction (passwords containing "cisco" in any case are rejected by LibreNMS), SNMPv3 credential completeness, alert transport type-specific required fields, alert rule transport reference integrity

docs.title: Security Best Practices
docs.text:
Production Security

Always use Ansible Vault for sensitive fields: - db_password - db_root_password - web_password - snmpv3_authpass - snmpv3_cryptopass - email_smtp_password - Alert transport API tokens

# Encrypt sensitive values
ansible-vault encrypt_string 'YourSecretPassword' --name 'db_password'

SNMP Community Strings

  • Never use default communities (public, private) in production
  • Use strong, unique community strings per device or device group
  • Consider SNMPv3 for devices that support it (better security)

Alert Configuration

  • Set is_default: true on your primary notification channel (email) to ensure no alerts are missed
  • Use count: 1 and interval: 0m for one-time alerting (recommended)
  • Add delay: 5m to rules that may have transient issues (e.g., high CPU)
  • Use the recovery: true flag to get notified when issues clear

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Device not being monitored: - Check SNMP is enabled on the target device - Verify community string or SNMPv3 credentials are correct - Test SNMP manually: snmpwalk -v2c -c <community> <device-ip> - Check LibreNMS logs: docker logs tbac_librenms

Alerts not being sent: - Verify transport configuration in LibreNMS web UI (Alerts → Alert Transports) - Check alert rules are enabled (Alerts → Alert Rules) - Test transport: LibreNMS UI → Alert Transports → Test - Review alert log: LibreNMS UI → Alerts → Alert Log

Web interface not accessible: - Verify containers are running: docker ps | grep librenms - Check web port is open: curl http://localhost:8000 - Review container logs: docker logs tbac_librenms

docs.title: TACACS+ Service
docs.text:
TACACS+ AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) service using the christianbecker/tac_plus-ng Docker image. Provides centralized authentication and authorization for network devices.

Network mode

TACACS+ uses bridge network mode with port mapping (49:49/tcp). It does not use host network mode.

LDAP/MAVIS backend

The deployment templates include a commented-out MAVIS/LDAP backend block. This requires the Testbed as Code LDAP service to be deployed on the same VM first. LDAP-backed authentication is not enabled by default.

docs.title: Supported Images
docs.text: Image Supported Versions Notes christianbecker/tac_plus-ng latest (default) tac_plus-ng daemon with spawnd process manager

docs.title: Basic TACACS+ Configuration
docs.text:
Minimal configuration to authenticate a network device with one local user:

network_services:
  tacacs:
    hosts:
      - name: "router01"
        address: "192.0.2.1"
        secret: !vault |
          $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256
          ...                          # Replace with vault-encrypted shared secret
    local_users:
      - name: "tbac"
        password: !vault |
          $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256
          ...                          # Replace with vault-encrypted password
        member: "full_access"
    profiles:
      - name: "full_access"
        attributes:
          shell:
            priv_level: 15             # Grant privilege level 15

docs.title: Complete TACACS+ Example
docs.text:
network_services:
  tacacs:
    service_name: "tbac_tacacs"        # Docker service/container name (default: tbac_tacacs)
    image_name: "christianbecker/tac_plus-ng"
    image_version: "latest"
    port: 49                           # TACACS+ standard port (default: 49)
    spawnd_instances_min: 1            # Minimum spawnd worker processes (default: 1)
    spawnd_instances_max: 10           # Maximum spawnd worker processes (default: 10)
    log_accounting: true               # Log accounting events (default: true)
    log_authentication: true           # Log authentication events (default: true)
    log_authorization: true            # Log authorization events (default: true)
    enable_secret: !vault |encrypted|  # Global enable password (optional, use vault)

    # NAS devices permitted to query this TACACS+ server
    hosts:
      - name: "router01"
        address: "192.0.2.1"           # IP address or CIDR of NAS device
        secret: !vault |encrypted|     # Shared secret — always use vault
        prompt: "Login: "              # Optional: custom login prompt
      - name: "switch-subnet"
        address: "192.0.2.0/24"        # CIDR to permit a subnet of NAS devices
        secret: !vault |encrypted|

    # Local user accounts for authentication
    local_users:
      - name: "tbac"
        password: !vault |encrypted|   # Login password — always use vault
        member: "full_access"          # Assign to an authorization profile
      - name: "readonly"
        password: !vault |encrypted|
        member: "read_only"

    # Authorization profiles (attribute sets assigned to users)
    profiles:
      - name: "full_access"
        attributes:
          shell:
            priv_level: 15             # Sets shell priv-lvl (Cisco IOS privilege level)
            max_priv_lvl: 15           # Sets max_priv_lvl
          custom_attributes:
            - name: "Viptela-User-Group"   # Vendor AV-pair name
              value: "netadmin"             # AV-pair value
              type: "mandatory"             # mandatory (set) or optional (add)
      - name: "read_only"
        attributes:
          shell:
            priv_level: 1
            max_priv_lvl: 1

    # Rulesets (optional: rule-based authorization)
    rulesets:
      - name: "permit_show"
        rules:
          - attribute: "cmd"
            value: "show"

docs.title: TACACS+ Field Reference
docs.text: Field Type Default Required Description service_name string tbac_tacacs No Docker service and container name container_name string tbac_tacacs No Docker container name override image_name string christianbecker/tac_plus-ng No Docker image image_version string latest No Docker image tag port integer (1-65535) 49 No TACACS+ listen port local_users list [] No Local TACACS+ user accounts hosts list [] No NAS devices permitted to query the server profiles list [] No Authorization attribute sets rulesets list [] No Authentication/authorization rule groups spawnd_instances_min integer (>=1) 1 No Minimum spawnd worker processes spawnd_instances_max integer (>=1) 10 No Maximum spawnd worker processes enable_secret string "" No Global enable-mode password (use Ansible Vault) log_accounting boolean true No Enable accounting log log_authentication boolean true No Enable authentication log log_authorization boolean true No Enable authorization log

docs.title: TACACS+ Host Fields
docs.text:
NAS devices (hosts) are the network devices permitted to send TACACS+ queries to this server:

Field Type Required Description name string Yes Label for this NAS entry (used in config only) address string Yes IP address or CIDR block of the NAS device secret string Yes Shared secret between NAS and TACACS+ server (use Ansible Vault) prompt string No Custom login prompt displayed to users on this NAS

docs.title: TACACS+ Local User Fields
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name string Yes Username password string No Login password (use Ansible Vault) enable_password string No Enable-mode password override member string No Authorization profile name (must match a profiles[].name)

docs.title: TACACS+ Profile Fields
docs.text:
Profiles define the authorization attributes granted to users who are members of that profile:

Field Type Required Description name string Yes Profile name — referenced by local_users[].member attributes object No AV-pair attributes for this profile

docs.title: Profile Attributes
docs.text: Field Type Description attributes.shell object Well-known shell AV-pairs attributes.shell.priv_level integer (0-15) Sets priv-lvl for shell authorization attributes.shell.max_priv_lvl integer (0-15) Sets max_priv_lvl for shell authorization attributes.custom_attributes list Custom/vendor AV-pairs

docs.title: Custom Attribute Fields
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name string Yes AV-pair name (e.g. Viptela-User-Group) value string Yes AV-pair value (e.g. netadmin) type string No mandatory (uses set, default) or optional (uses add)

docs.title: Deployment Details
docs.text:
• Network mode: Bridge with port mapping 49:49/tcp (not host mode)
  • Config file: tac_plus-ng.cfg rendered to <docker_compose_dir>/tacacs/tac_plus-ng.cfg
  • Log directory: <docker_compose_dir>/tacacs/logs/
  • Verification: Deployment checks that the tbac_tacacs container reaches running status

docs.title: Relevant Files
docs.text:
• roles/deploy/tasks/service_deployment/tacacs/configure.yaml — directory creation, config rendering
  • roles/deploy/tasks/service_deployment/tacacs/deploy.yaml — Docker image pull, container start
  • roles/deploy/tasks/service_deployment/tacacs/verify.yaml — container status check
  • roles/deploy/templates/tacacs/tac_plus-ng.cfg.j2 — tac_plus-ng daemon configuration template
  • roles/deploy/templates/tacacs/docker-compose.yml.j2 — Docker Compose definition

docs.title: Security Best Practices
docs.text:
Always vault-encrypt secrets

The following TACACS+ fields must always be vault-encrypted before committing: - hosts[].secret — shared secret between NAS and TACACS+ server - local_users[].password — user login passwords - enable_secret — global enable password

ansible-vault encrypt_string 'MySharedSecret' --name 'secret'

Restrict host addresses

Use specific IP addresses in hosts[].address rather than 0.0.0.0/0. Each NAS device should have its own entry with the exact source IP.

docs.title: TFTP Service
docs.text:
TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol) service for network booting, firmware updates, and configuration file distribution.

Host Network Mode Only

TFTP uses host network mode exclusively. Bridge mode is not supported due to NAT issues with TFTP's dynamic UDP ports.

docs.title: Supported Images and Versions
docs.text:
Image Supported Versions Notes pghalliday/tftp latest Default image, full tftpd-hpa features lightnear/tftp latest Lightweight alternative (tftpd-hpa 5.2)

Version Validation

Testbed as Code validates Docker image names and versions against a whitelist and optionally checks Docker Hub for tag existence.

docs.title: Basic TFTP Configuration
docs.text:
network_services:
  tftp:
    tftp_address: "10.196.209.120"    # Bind to specific IP

docs.title: Complete TFTP Example
docs.text:
network_services:
  tftp:
    # Service identification (optional - defaults to 'tftp')
    service_name: "tftp"              # Service/directory name

    # Docker image configuration
    image_name: "pghalliday/tftp"     # Supported: pghalliday/tftp, lightnear/tftp
    image_version: "latest"           # Image tag (validated against Docker Hub)

    # TFTP server settings (optional — defaults provided)
    tftp_docker_root: "/var/tftpboot"        # Root directory inside container
    tftp_boot_dir: "/tftpboot"               # Local directory name for TFTP boot files
    tftp_address: "0.0.0.0"                   # Bind to all interfaces (or specific IP)
    tftp_port: 69                             # Standard TFTP port

    # Performance tuning (optional — defaults provided)
    max_block_size: 1468              # Maximum block size (optimized for MTU)
    timeout: 0                        # Timeout in seconds (0 = disabled)
    retransmit: 0                     # Retransmit attempts (0 = unlimited)

    # Security options (optional — defaults provided)
    secure_mode: true                 # Enable chroot to tftp_docker_root (recommended)
    create_mode: true                 # Allow client uploads
    permissive_mode: false            # Strict file access mode

    # Logging (optional — default provided)
    verbosity: 1                      # 0=quiet, 1=normal, 2=verbose, 3=debug

    # Note: Directory permissions hardcoded to 0755 (rwxr-xr-x)

    # Custom tftpd-hpa options (optional)
    tftp_options: []                  # e.g., ["--ipv4", "--map-file=/etc/tftpd.map"]

docs.title: TFTP Field Reference
docs.text: Field Type Default Required Description service_name string tftp No Service/directory name image_name string pghalliday/tftp No Docker image for TFTP service image_version string latest Yes Docker image tag tftp_docker_root string /var/tftpboot No Root directory for TFTP files inside container tftp_boot_dir string /tftpboot No Local directory name for TFTP boot files tftp_address IPv4/string 0.0.0.0 No Address to bind TFTP server (IPv4, localhost, or 0.0.0.0) tftp_port integer 69 No UDP port for TFTP service (1-65535) max_block_size integer 1468 No Maximum TFTP block size (512-65464 bytes) timeout integer 0 No Timeout in seconds before retransmit (0 = disabled) retransmit integer 0 No Retransmit attempts (0 = unlimited) secure_mode boolean true No Enable secure mode (chroot to tftp_docker_root) create_mode boolean true No Allow file uploads from clients verbosity integer 1 No Logging verbosity (0=quiet, 1=normal, 2=verbose, 3=debug) permissive_mode boolean false No Relax file permission checks tftp_options list [] No Additional tftpd-hpa command-line options environment_vars list [] No Docker environment variables command_args list [] No Custom Docker command arguments entrypoint any - No Custom Docker entrypoint

docs.title: Directory Permissions
docs.text: Directory permissions are hardcoded to 0755 (rwxr-xr-x) to ensure proper directory traversal.

docs.title: Network Mode
docs.text:
TFTP uses host network mode only (hardcoded in docker-compose template).

Mode Description host Container uses host's network stack directly

Why Host Mode Only?

  • TFTP uses dynamic UDP ports that don't work with NAT/port mapping
  • Bridge mode causes reliability issues with TFTP transfers
  • Host mode provides direct network access without NAT overhead

docs.title: File Storage
docs.text:
Location Description Host VM <docker_compose_dir>/<service_name>/<tftp_boot_dir>/ Inside Container /var/tftpboot/ (configurable via tftp_docker_root)

Default paths (using defaults.yaml values):

Location Default Path Host VM /opt/net-services/tftp/tftpboot/ Inside Container /var/tftpboot/

Files placed on the host VM are automatically available inside the container via Docker volume mount.

# Upload files to TFTP server
scp firmware.bin ubuntu@<VM_IP>:/opt/net-services/tftp/tftpboot/

# Or create files directly on VM
ssh ubuntu@<VM_IP>
sudo cp /path/to/file /opt/net-services/tftp/tftpboot/

TFTP Boot Files

Place boot files (PXE, firmware, configs) in the tftpboot directory on the TFTP server VM. Files must have appropriate read permissions for the TFTP daemon.

Security Considerations

  • Keep secure_mode: true in production to prevent directory traversal
  • Only enable create_mode: true if client uploads are required
  • Use firewall rules to restrict TFTP access to trusted networks

docs.title: Data Model
docs.text: Complete reference for the Testbed as Code data model structure.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Testbed as Code data model provides a declarative, YAML-based approach to defining virtual machine testbed infrastructure. The model follows Network as Code principles with a hierarchical structure that separates infrastructure concerns into logical sections.

docs.title: Data Model Structure
docs.text: The data model is organized into five main configuration areas:

docs.title: Hypervisor
docs.text: Infrastructure deployment targets including vCenter servers, ESXi hosts, and future platform support (OpenStack, Proxmox, Nutanix).

docs.title: Compute
docs.text: Virtual machine definitions including hardware specifications, network configuration, and OS customization.

docs.title: Network Services
docs.text: Network infrastructure services (DNS, DHCP, NTP, TFTP, HTTP, LDAP, TACACS+, SNMP/LibreNMS) with Docker-based deployment configuration.

docs.title: Application Services
docs.text: Application deployments for services like Netbox, Splunk, and custom applications (future).

docs.title: Images
docs.text: Centralized image catalog for OVA and ISO files with authentication and version management.

docs.title: Root Structure
docs.text:
The root testbed object contains metadata and references to the five main sections:

testbed:
  name: str                    # Testbed identifier (optional)
  description: str             # Purpose description (optional)
  type: enum('shared', 'dmz')  # Testbed type (optional)
  pyats: {...}                 # Optional: pyATS integration settings

  hypervisor: {...}            # Required: Infrastructure targets
  compute: {...}               # Optional: VM definitions
  network_services: {...}      # Optional: Network services
  application_services: {...}  # Optional: Applications
  images: [...]                # Optional: Image catalog

docs.title: Root Fields
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name string No Testbed identifier for reference description string No Human-readable description of testbed purpose type enum No Testbed type: shared (multi-user) or dmz (isolated) pyats object No pyATS integration settings (see pyATS reference) hypervisor object Yes Infrastructure deployment targets compute object No Virtual machine definitions network_services object No DNS, DHCP, NTP, TFTP, HTTP, LDAP, TACACS+, SNMP/LibreNMS service configurations application_services object No Application service definitions images list No Centralized image catalog

docs.title: Data Model Validation
docs.text: All configurations are validated against the YAMALE schema before deployment:

docs.title: Validation Flow
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[YAML Config] --> B[Schema Validation]
    B --> C[Semantic Validation]
    C --> D[Deployment]

    style A fill:#049fd9,color:#fff
    style B fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#00c853,color:#fff
  1. Schema Validation: YAMALE checks structure, types, required fields
  2. Semantic Validation: Python rules verify business logic and relationships
  3. Deployment: Configuration applied to infrastructure

Learn more about Validation →

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
Here's a complete testbed configuration showing all sections:

testbed:
  name: "production-lab"
  description: "Production testing environment"
  type: "shared"

  hypervisor:
    vcenter:
      - host: "vcenter.lab.local"
        username: "admin@vsphere.local"
        password: !vault |encrypted|
        validate_certs: false
        datacenters:
          - name: "Lab-DC"
            clusters:
              - name: "Cluster1"
                datastore: "shared-storage"
                vm_names: ["tbac_vm01", "tbac_vm02"]

  images:
    - name: "tbac-base-ubuntu"
      type: "ova"
      version: "1.0.0"
      artifact:
        location: "artifactory"
        path: "NetworkServices/TBAC/tbac_2404-1.ova"
        url: "https://artifactory.example.com/generic-local"
        auth:
          type: "bearer_token"
          token: !vault |encrypted|

  compute:
    vms:
      - name: "tbac_vm01"
        deploy_from: "ova"
        ova_image_name: "tbac-base-ubuntu"
        folder: "/production"
        hardware:
          cpu: 4
          memory_mb: 8192
          disk_gb: 100
        network:
          interfaces:
            - name: "ens192"
              portgroup: "VLAN-100"
              ip: "192.168.1.10"
              netmask: "255.255.255.0"
              gateway: "192.168.1.1"
        os_config:
          hostname: "tbac_vm01"
          dns: ["8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4"]
          domain: "lab.local"
          timezone: "America/New_York"
          users:
            - username: "admin"
              password: "CHANGE_ME_USE_VAULT"
              sudo: true
        services:
          dns:
            enable: true

  network_services:
    dns:
      upstream_servers: ["8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4"]
      a_records:
        - name: "web.local."
          type: "A"
          value: "192.168.1.10"

    dhcp:
      interfaces: ["ens192"]
      subnets:
        - cidr: "192.168.1.0/24"
          range_start: "192.168.1.100"
          range_end: "192.168.1.200"
          router: "192.168.1.1"
          dns_servers: ["192.168.1.10"]

    ntp:
      allow_networks: ["192.168.1.0/24"]
      upstream_servers:
        - "0.pool.ntp.org"
        - name: "time.google.com"
          options: "iburst prefer"

  application_services: {}

docs.title: Compute
docs.text:
Data model key: compute

Defines virtual machine specifications, deployment methods, hardware, networking, and OS configuration.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
compute:
  vms:
    - name: "tbac_vm01"
      deploy_from: "ova"
      folder: "/my-vms"
      template: {...}          # Template deployment config
      ova_image_name: "..."    # Image catalog reference

      network: {...}
      os_config: {...}

docs.title: Per-VM Service Enablement
docs.text:
services:
  dns:
    enable: true
  dhcp:
    enable: false

applications:
  netbox:
    enable: true

services vs applications

Use services for network services (DNS, DHCP, NTP, TFTP, HTTP, TACACS+, LDAP, SNMP/LibreNMS). Use applications for application services (Netbox). The two blocks are separate because application services involve multi-container stacks with database migrations, while network services are single-container deployments.

docs.title: Per-VM Enablement
docs.text:
Application services are enabled per-VM using the applications block (distinct from services which controls network services):

compute:
  vms:
    - name: tbac_vm01
      # ...
      applications:
        netbox:
          enable: true

Note

The applications key is separate from services. Network services (DNS, DHCP, NTP, etc.) are controlled under services. Application services (Netbox, etc.) are controlled under applications.

docs.title: Developer Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the Testbed as Code developer guide. This section is for contributors and developers who want to extend or modify the project.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Testbed as Code is built with extensibility in mind. Whether you're fixing bugs, adding features, or creating custom plugins, this guide will help you get started.

docs.title: Quick Setup
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone git@github.com:cxsvs-int/svs-testbed-as-code.git
cd svs-testbed-as-code

# Set up development environment with UV (recommended)
./setup_uv.sh

# Or with pyenv
./setup.sh

# Install development dependencies
uv pip install pytest pytest-cov ansible-lint yamllint

# Run tests to verify setup
uv run pytest tests/

Full Setup Guide →

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: We welcome contributions! Here's how to get started:

docs.title: Contributing Workflow
docs.text:
Learn the branch naming, commit conventions, and PR process.

Contributing Guide →

docs.text:
Understand the test structure and how to write tests.

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
testbed-as-code/
├── plugins/action/              # Custom Ansible plugins
│   ├── *.py                     # Plugin entry points
│   └── common/                  # Shared plugin logic
├── roles/                       # Ansible roles
│   ├── common/                  # Base role (merge, pyATS)
│   ├── validate/                # Validation role
│   ├── deploy/                  # Deployment role
│   ├── operate/                 # Runtime operations role (LDAP)
│   └── remove/                  # Cleanup role (planned)
├── tests/                       # Test suite
│   ├── unit/                    # Unit tests
│   └── integration/             # Integration tests
└── schema/                      # YAMALE schemas

docs.title: Key Technologies
docs.text:
• Ansible 2.19+: Automation engine
  • Python 3.12+: Plugin and validation logic
  • YAMALE: Schema validation
  • PyVmomi: VMware API bindings
  • Jinja2: Template rendering
  • pytest: Test framework

docs.title: Adding a New Feature
docs.text:
1. Create a feature branch: git checkout -b TICKET-ID-feature/brief-description
  2. Implement the feature: Code, tests, documentation
  3. Run tests: uv run pytest tests/
  4. Run linting: uv run ansible-lint
  5. Submit PR: Against develop branch

docs.title: Fixing a Bug
docs.text:
1. Create a fix branch: git checkout -b TICKET-ID-fix/brief-description
  2. Write a failing test: Reproduce the bug
  3. Fix the bug: Make minimal changes
  4. Verify fix: Ensure tests pass
  5. Submit PR: Against develop branch

docs.title: Adding a Network Service
docs.text:
1. Update data model: Add service to schema/schema.yaml
  2. Create deployment tasks: In roles/deploy/tasks/service_deployment/
  3. Add validation rules: In roles/validate/files/rules/
  4. Write tests: Unit and integration tests
  5. Document: Update docs and examples

Detailed Service Guide →

docs.title: Plugin Architecture
docs.text:
# plugins/action/my_plugin.py (entry point)
from ansible.plugins.action import ActionBase
from .common.my_plugin import execute_my_plugin

class ActionModule(ActionBase):
    def run(self, tmp=None, task_vars=None):
        # Delegate to common logic
        return execute_my_plugin(task_vars)

# plugins/action/common/my_plugin.py (logic)
def execute_my_plugin(task_vars):
    """Reusable plugin logic with comprehensive error handling."""
    # Implementation here
    pass

Benefits:

  • Separation of Ansible interface from business logic
  • Easier unit testing
  • Code reuse across plugins

docs.title: Role Structure
docs.text:
Standard Ansible role structure:

roles/my_role/
├── tasks/
│   └── main.yaml        # Entry point
├── defaults/
│   └── main.yaml        # Default variables
├── meta/
│   └── main.yaml        # Dependencies
├── templates/           # Jinja2 templates
├── files/               # Static files
└── README.md            # Role documentation

docs.title: Test Coverage Requirements
docs.text:
All contributions must include tests:

  • Unit tests: For plugins and utilities (>80% coverage)
  • Integration tests: For end-to-end workflows
  • Ansible tests: For role functionality

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
# Run all tests
uv run pytest tests/ -v

# Run specific test file
uv run pytest tests/unit/plugins/action/common/test_yaml_merge_utils.py -v

# Run with coverage
uv run pytest tests/ --cov=plugins --cov-report=html

# Run integration tests
ansible-playbook tests/integration/merge/test_merge.yaml -v

Complete Testing Guide →

Install pre-commit hooks for automatic checks:

docs.title: Code Standards
docs.text: Coding standards and best practices for Testbed as Code.

docs.title: File Extensions
docs.text:
• Use .yaml for all Ansible files (not .yml)
  • Jinja2 templates: .yml.j2 or .yaml.j2

docs.title: Path Conventions
docs.text:
Always use Ansible reserved variables:

# ✅ Correct
lookup('template', role_path ~ '/templates/file.j2')
project_root: "{{ playbook_dir | dirname }}"

# ❌ Incorrect
lookup('template', '../../templates/file.j2')
project_root: "{{ playbook_dir }}/.."

docs.title: Module Names
docs.text:
Always use fully qualified collection names (FQCN):

# ✅ Correct
- community.vmware.vmware_guest:

# ❌ Incorrect
- vmware_guest:

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text:
Follow PEP 8:

# Function definitions
def merge_yaml_objects(defaults, data):
    """Merge two YAML objects intelligently."""
    pass

# Class definitions
class ValidationResult:
    """Validation result container."""
    pass

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Use docstrings:

def merge_lists(list1, list2):
    """
    Merge two lists with smart deduplication.

    Args:
        list1: First list
        list2: Second list

    Returns:
        Merged list with duplicates removed
    """
    pass

docs.title: Testing Standards
docs.text:
• Write tests for all new code
  • Achieve >80% coverage
  • Use descriptive test names
  • Include edge cases

docs.title: Documentation Standards
docs.text:
• Update relevant docs with changes
  • Add examples for new features
  • Use clear, concise language
  • Include code snippets

docs.title: Commit Messages
docs.text:
Use plain sentence style -- no conventional commit prefixes (feat:, fix:, etc.).

# Correct
Add pyATS testbed generation
Fix path resolution issue in merge plugin
Update merge system documentation

# Incorrect
feat: add pyATS testbed generation
fix: resolve path resolution issue

Keep commits atomic -- one logical change per commit.

docs.title: Plugin Development
docs.text: Create custom Ansible action plugins for Testbed as Code.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Action plugins extend Ansible functionality with custom Python code.

docs.title: Plugin Structure
docs.text:
# plugins/action/my_plugin.py
from ansible.plugins.action import ActionBase
from .common.my_plugin import execute_plugin

class ActionModule(ActionBase):
    def run(self, tmp=None, task_vars=None):
        result = super(ActionModule, self).run(tmp, task_vars)
        return execute_plugin(self, task_vars)

# plugins/action/common/my_plugin.py
def execute_plugin(action_module, task_vars):
    """Plugin implementation."""
    return {"changed": False, "msg": "Success"}

docs.title: Creating a New Plugin
docs.text:
1. Create plugin entry point in plugins/action/
  2. Implement logic in plugins/action/common/
  3. Write unit tests

        url: "https://artifactory.devhub-cloud.cisco.com/artifactory/cx-svs-cdn-generic"

docs.title: Key Configuration Points
docs.text:
vCenter Connection (hypervisor.vcenter):

  • Replace vcenter.lab.local with your vCenter hostname
  • Use your admin username
  • Encrypt your password with ansible-vault encrypt_string

Datacenter/Cluster (datacenters, clusters):

  • Match your vCenter datacenter name exactly
  • Match your cluster name exactly
  • Specify correct datastore name

Image Path (images[0].artifact.path):

  • Use absolute path to your OVA file
  • Or use HTTP URL: https://cloud-images.ubuntu.com/...

Network Settings (network.interfaces):

  • Match your vSphere port group name
  • Use available IP address
  • Set correct gateway and netmask

# Deploy the testbed
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml

The deployment process:

  1. ✅ Merges configuration (common role)
  2. ✅ Validates schema and semantics (validate role)

Deployment takes approximately 10-15 minutes depending on OVA size and network speed.

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
OVA Upload Failed

Error: Cannot upload OVA to vCenter

Solutions: - Verify OVA file path is correct - Check network connectivity to vCenter - Ensure sufficient datastore space - Try with smaller OVA file first

VM Won't Boot

Error: VM deployed but won't power on

Solutions: - Check ESXi host has sufficient resources - Verify network port group exists - Review vCenter task history for errors - Check ansible.log for details

Can't SSH to VM

Error: Connection refused on port 22

Solutions: - Wait 2-3 minutes for cloud-init to complete - Verify IP address is correct and reachable - Check VM console for boot errors - Verify SSH key is correct (if using key auth)

tail -f ansible.log

#   -e "vm_delete_mode=true"

Destructive Operation

The remove operation will permanently delete VMs. Always verify you're targeting the correct testbed.

docs.title: Network Services Example
docs.text: Deploy complete network infrastructure with DNS, DHCP, NTP, TFTP, HTTP, TACACS+, LDAP, and SNMP/LibreNMS monitoring services.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This example shows how to set up a complete network services infrastructure including all eight supported Docker-based services.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
testbed:
  name: "network-services-lab"

  compute:
    vms:
      - name: "tbac_vm01"
        deploy_from: "ova"
        ova_image_name: "tbac-base-ubuntu"
        services:
          dns:
            enable: true
          dhcp:
            enable: true
          ntp:
            enable: true
          tftp:
            enable: true
          http:
            enable: true
          tacacs:
            enable: true
          ldap:
            enable: true
          snmp:
            enable: true

  network_services:
    dns:
      image_name: "mvance/unbound"
      image_version: "1.21.1"
      upstream_servers: ["8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4"]
      a_records:
        - name: "web.local."
          type: "A"
          value: "192.168.1.10"

    dhcp:
      image_name: "kosdk/dhcpd"
      image_version: "4.4.1-2.3"
      interfaces: ["ens192"]
      subnets:
        - cidr: "192.168.1.0/24"
          range_start: "192.168.1.100"
          range_end: "192.168.1.200"
          router: "192.168.1.1"

    ntp:
      image_name: "cturra/ntp"
      image_version: "latest"
      version: 4
      upstream_servers:
        - "0.pool.ntp.org"

    tftp:
      image_name: "pghalliday/tftp"
      image_version: "latest"
      tftp_docker_root: "/var/tftpboot"
      tftp_boot_dir: "/tftpboot"
      tftp_address: "0.0.0.0"
      tftp_port: 69
      max_block_size: 1468
      timeout: 1
      retransmit: 0
      create_mode: true
      secure_mode: true
      verbosity: 1
      permissive_mode: false

    http:
      image_name: "caddy"
      image_version: "alpine"
      http_port: 80
      https_port: 443
      root_dir: "http"
      sites:
        - name: "files"
          domain: "files.lab.local"
          root: "shared"
          browse: true

    tacacs:
      image_name: "christianbecker/tac_plus-ng"
      image_version: "latest"
      hosts:
        - name: "router01"
          address: "192.168.1.1"
          secret: !vault |
            $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256
            ...
      local_users:
        - name: "tbac"
          password: !vault |
            $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256
            ...
          member: "full_access"
      profiles:
        - name: "full_access"
          attributes:
            shell:
              priv_level: 15

    ldap:
      image_name: "bitnami/openldap"
      image_version: "2.6"
      base_dn: "dc=lab,dc=local"
      admin_password: !vault |
        $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256
        ...
      seed_ous:
        - People
        - Groups
      seed_groups:
        - cn: ldapusers
          gid_number: 5000
      seed_users:
        - uid: tbac-admin
          cn: "TBaC Admin"
          sn: "Admin"
          uid_number: 10001
          gid_number: 5000
          home: /home/tbac-admin
          shell: /bin/bash
          password: !vault |
            $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256
            ...

    snmp:
      service_name: "tbac_librenms"
      image_name: "librenms/librenms"
      image_version: "26.2.0"
      db_password: "secure_db_password"      # Change in production (use ansible-vault)
      db_root_password: "secure_root_pwd"    # Change in production (use ansible-vault)
      web_password: "admin_password"         # Web UI login (use ansible-vault)
      snmp_community: "private"
      timezone: "UTC"
      web_port: 8000
      poller_frequency: 300                  # Alert evaluation interval in seconds (default 300)
      fast_ping: true                        # Detect device down in 60s
      fast_ping_interval: 60
      alert_default_only: false          # false = use per-rule transports list
      alert_transports:
        - name: "Email Ops Team"
          type: "mail"
          is_default: true
          config:
            email: "ops@example.com"
            email_backend: smtp
            email_smtp_host: smtp.example.com
            email_smtp_port: 25
            email_from: '"LibreNMS" <librenms@example.com>'
      alert_rules:
        - name: "Port Down"
          severity: critical
          conditions:
            - field: ports.ifOperStatus
              operator: "="
              value: "down"
            - field: ports.ifAdminStatus
              operator: "="
              value: "up"
          transports: ["Email Ops Team"]
        - name: "Device Down"
          severity: critical
          conditions:
            - field: devices.status
              operator: "="
              value: "0"
          transports: ["Email Ops Team"]
      monitored_devices:
        - hostname: "192.168.1.1"
          community: "private"
          snmp_version: "v2c"
          display_name: "Router1"
        - hostname: "192.168.1.2"
          snmp_version: "v3"
          snmpv3_authname: "snmpv3user"
          snmpv3_authpass: "authpassword123"    # Use ansible-vault in production
          snmpv3_authalgo: "SHA"
          snmpv3_cryptopass: "privpassword456"  # Use ansible-vault in production
          snmpv3_cryptoalgo: "AES"
          display_name: "Switch1"
          # Auth level (authPriv) is auto-detected from provided credentials

docs.title: Alert Rule Behavior
docs.text:
By default, TBaC configures alert rules for one-time notification:

Setting Default Behavior count 1 Alert fires once per event interval 0m No re-alerting recovery true Sends recovery email when device/port comes back up

This means: one email when down → one email when recovered.

To override (e.g. re-alert every 15 minutes up to 5 times):

alert_rules:
  - name: "Device Down"
    severity: critical
    count: 5           # Re-alert up to 5 times
    interval: "15m"    # Every 15 minutes
    recovery: true
    conditions:
      - field: devices.status
        operator: "="
        value: "0"
    transports: ["Email Ops Team"]

docs.title: Slack Transport
docs.text:
network_services:
  snmp:
    alert_default_only: false
    alert_transports:
      - name: "Slack NOC"
        type: "slack"
        config:
          url: "https://hooks.slack.com/services/T00000/B00000/XXXXXXX"
          channel: "#network-alerts"   # Optional: override webhook default channel
    alert_rules:
      - name: "Device Down"
        severity: critical
        conditions:
          - field: devices.status
            operator: "="
            value: "0"
        transports: ["Slack NOC"]

docs.title: Microsoft Teams Transport
docs.text:
network_services:
  snmp:
    alert_default_only: false
    alert_transports:
      - name: "Teams NOC Channel"
        type: "microsoft_teams"
        config:
          url: "https://outlook.office.com/webhook/YOUR-WEBHOOK-URL"
          use-json: false              # Set true for JSON MessageCard format
    alert_rules:
      - name: "Port Down"
        severity: critical
        conditions:
          - field: ports.ifOperStatus
            operator: "="
            value: "down"
          - field: ports.ifAdminStatus
            operator: "="
            value: "up"
        transports: ["Teams NOC Channel"]

docs.title: Cisco Webex Teams Transport
docs.text:
network_services:
  snmp:
    alert_default_only: true              # true = only send via is_default transports
    alert_transports:
      - name: "Webex NOC Space"
        type: "cisco_webex_teams"
        is_default: true               # Apply to ALL alert rules
        config:
          api-token: "NjE2YzY5..."     # Bot access token from developer.webex.com
          room-id: "Y2lzY29zcGFyay..." # Room ID from GET /v1/rooms
          use-markdown: false           # Enable Markdown formatting
    alert_rules:
      - name: "Device Down"
        severity: critical
        conditions:
          - field: devices.status
            operator: "="
            value: "0"
        # No transports needed — Webex has is_default: true

docs.title: Webhook (API) Transport
docs.text:
network_services:
  snmp:
    alert_default_only: false
    alert_transports:
      - name: "PagerDuty"
        type: "webhook"
        config:
          url: "https://events.pagerduty.com/generic/2010-04-15/create_event.json"
          method: "POST"               # GET, POST, or PUT (default: POST)
    alert_rules:
      - name: "Device Down"
        severity: critical
        conditions:
          - field: devices.status
            operator: "="
            value: "0"
        transports: ["PagerDuty"]

docs.title: Multiple Transports (Email + Slack + Webex)
docs.text:
network_services:
  snmp:
    alert_default_only: false
    alert_transports:
      - name: "Email Ops"
        type: "mail"
        is_default: true
        config:
          email: "ops@example.com"
          email_backend: smtp
          email_smtp_host: smtp.example.com
      - name: "Slack NOC"
        type: "slack"
        config:
          url: "https://hooks.slack.com/services/T00000/B00000/XXXXXXX"
      - name: "Webex NOC"
        type: "cisco_webex_teams"
        config:
          api-token: "NjE2YzY5..."
          room-id: "Y2lzY29zcGFyay..."
    alert_rules:
      - name: "Port Down"
        severity: critical
        conditions:
          - field: ports.ifOperStatus
            operator: "="
            value: "down"
          - field: ports.ifAdminStatus
            operator: "="
            value: "up"
        transports: ["Slack NOC", "Webex NOC"]
        # Email Ops has is_default: true, so it receives ALL alerts automatically

This example demonstrates a production-ready deployment with:

  • High availability considerations

docs.title: Configuration Approach
docs.text:
Use multi-file configuration with defaults:

data-prod/
├── hypervisor.tbac.yaml
├── services.tbac.yaml

# Deploy to production
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml -e "data_dir=data-prod"

1. Test in staging first
