docs.title: Getting Started with Testbed as Code
docs.text: Welcome to Testbed as Code! This guide will help you get up and running quickly with automated infrastructure testing.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Testbed as Code provides a simple, declarative way to define and deploy virtual machine testbeds with integrated network services. Whether you're setting up a small lab environment or a complex multi-VM testing infrastructure, Testbed as Code makes it easy.

docs.title: What You'll Need
docs.text:
Before you begin, make sure you have:

  • Access to VMware Infrastructure: vCenter Server or ESXi hosts with administrative/write access (permissions to create/deploy new Virtual Machines)
  • Python 3.12+: Required for running Ansible and the toolchain (can be installed via our setup.sh scripts)
  • macOS or Linux: Windows is not currently supported

docs.title: Installation Path
docs.text:
Follow these steps to get started:

  1. Prerequisites - Set up your environment and gather necessary access credentials
  2. Quick Start - Clone, configure, and deploy your first testbed end-to-end
  3. Installation - Detailed installation and setup reference
  4. Configuration - Detailed field-by-field configuration reference

docs.title: Quick Overview
docs.text:
Here's what the typical workflow looks like:

graph LR
    A[Define Testbed] --> B[Validate Config]
    B --> C[Deploy VMs]
    C --> D[Configure Services]
    D --> E[Test & Verify]

    style A fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff9000,color:#fff
  1. Define your testbed in YAML
  2. Validate the configuration
  3. Deploy VMs to your infrastructure
  4. Configure network services
  5. Test with pyATS integration

docs.title: Data-Driven Approach
docs.text:
Everything is defined in YAML files. No programming required:

testbed:
  name: "my-lab"
  hypervisor:
    vcenter: [...]
  compute:
    vms: [...]
  network_services: [...]

docs.title: Role-Based Architecture
docs.text:
Testbed as Code uses a modular architecture:

  • common - Merges configuration files
  • validate - Checks configuration correctness
  • deploy - Provisions infrastructure
  • operate - Day-2 runtime operations (LDAP password resets, user/group sync)
  • remove - Safely cleans up resources (planned)

docs.title: Safety First
docs.text:
Built-in protections prevent accidental deletion:

  • Vault-encrypted credentials
  • Validation before deployment
  • Explicit safety flags for destructive operations
  • Change tracking and rollback support

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Ready to begin? Continue to Prerequisites to set up your environment.

Need Help?

  • Check the FAQ for common questions
  • Review Examples for inspiration
  • See Troubleshooting for debugging tips

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Learn how to create your first testbed configuration.

docs.title: Configuration Basics
docs.text:
Testbed as Code uses YAML files to define your infrastructure. You can choose between:

  1. Single-file approach: Everything in one file
  2. Multi-file approach: Organized with defaults and data files (recommended)

docs.title: Your First Configuration
docs.text: Let's create a simple testbed with one VM.

docs.title: Step 1: Create Data File
docs.text:
Create data/testbed.tbac.yaml:

testbed:
  name: "my-first-lab"
  type: "shared"

  hypervisor:
    vcenter:
      - host: "vcenter.lab.local"
        username: "administrator@vsphere.local"
        password: !vault |
          $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256
          66386439663064313539653430376430613438646232626233653265373263613864646637353435
          6230613833656537373032323734383133366366336264640a663538373833643164346134636334
          36636439313433333439323162653238393038633331613165336235386436646466663064393739
          6534653237616637610a383866343135663739383366363633333631623830306237383231333039
          3864
        validate_certs: false
        datacenters:
          - name: "Lab-DC"
            clusters:
              - name: "Lab-Cluster"
                datastore: "shared-storage"
                vm_names: ["tbac_vm01"]

  images:
    - name: "tbac-base-ubuntu"
      type: "ova"
      version: "1.0.0"
      artifact:
        location: "artifactory"
        path: "NetworkServices/TBAC/tbac_2404-1.ova"
        url: "https://artifactory.devhub-cloud.cisco.com/artifactory/cx-svs-cdn-generic"
        checksum_sha256: "30d7063a685ddc11fe1b3e92c904bee6103a609ed1c6bc5431b8bd6fe1d04fb7"
        auth:
          type: "bearer_token"
          token: "eyJ2ZXIiOiIyIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIiwiYWxnIjoiUlMyNTYiLCJraWQiOiJicC1uNDZ5dnpGVGdYZmVSR0JkVUgxaW1WMTBrZnplT3lrZ1BQRmItVVhJIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJqZmFjQDAxZzNoOThtOTUzMmZ5MHpuMG5neDUxMDczL3VzZXJzL3N2c2F1dG8uZ2VuIiwic2NwIjoiYXBwbGllZC1wZXJtaXNzaW9ucy91c2VyIiwiYXVkIjoiKkAqIiwiaXNzIjoiamZhY0AwMWczaDk4bTk1MzJmeTB6bjBuZ3g1MTA3MyIsImV4cCI6MTc4Mjk5NjEwOCwiaWF0IjoxNzc1MjIwMTA4LCJqdGkiOiJmMmYxMmMyZi0yMjZiLTQ0MjYtOWY2Ny02NWY1ODZhNWE3ZWQifQ.MbgaLZM-KdvZn5Vmg9Hnxdv77DhjL2oOryBGDQ1kgryDIvR0ScsXB6nkJzuScGkY1P4x7yeh98nu7woNLUDEh0GLzOwHGJJI-paONvjMcp_yPjFDSSolwKocYj8E0CtPynkbIu0FRunwjtcjxdeKa0TpgTqx6SmyC5l2YV1OeIok7m1G7bi6QVPSvcTis0pdVzr7c4d2LzicRXaSX5I0nsqGTDLtpNxpYABaEdP7XtL334CXQe4Wggqcv0h-wMVQhahA4qmp6QOiuhJ9OBjujPt7NBZUxOOlK1DfGW76UUJI6vnudhWZA9zH6f0MsBuO3ORxMNUOAFkOPyl4LrL20A"

  compute:
    vms:
      - name: "tbac_vm01"
        deploy_from: "ova"
        ova_image_name: "tbac-base-ubuntu"
        hardware:
          cpu: 2
          memory_mb: 4096
          disk_gb: 40
        network:
          interfaces:
            - name: "ens192"
              portgroup: "VM Network"
              ip: "192.168.1.100"
              netmask: "255.255.255.0"
              gateway: "192.168.1.1"
        os_config:
          hostname: "tbac_vm01"
          dns: ["8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4"]
          users:                               # Additional users (default 'ubuntu'/'cisco.123' is baked into OVA)
            - username: "tbac"
              password: "CHANGE_ME_USE_VAULT"  # Use ansible-vault encrypt_string
              sudo: true
              allow_password_auth: true
              ssh_public_key_file: "~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub"
              ssh_private_key_file: "~/.ssh/id_ed25519"

Default VM Credentials

VMs deployed from the TBaC base OVA image include a built-in default user. See Default VM Credentials for details. The os_config.users block above defines additional users created on first boot via cloud-init.

docs.title: Step 2: Encrypt Credentials
docs.text:
Never store passwords in plain text. Encrypt them with Ansible Vault:

# Create vault password file
echo 'MySecureVaultPassword123!' > .vault_pass
chmod 600 .vault_pass

# Encrypt your vCenter password
ansible-vault encrypt_string 'YourVCenterPassword' --name 'password'

Copy the output (starting with !vault |) and paste it as the password value in your YAML.

docs.title: Step 3: Customize for Your Environment
docs.text:
Update these values to match your setup:

Field Example Your Value hypervisor.vcenter[0].host vcenter.lab.local Your vCenter FQDN hypervisor.vcenter[0].username administrator@vsphere.local Your admin user datacenters[0].name Lab-DC Your datacenter name clusters[0].name Lab-Cluster Your cluster name datastore shared-storage Your datastore name images[0].artifact.path /path/to/ova Path to your OVA file network.interfaces[0].portgroup VM Network Your port group name network.interfaces[0].ip 192.168.1.100 Your VM IP address

docs.title: Hypervisor Section
docs.text:
Defines where VMs will be deployed:

hypervisor:
  vcenter:              # For vCenter deployment
    - host: "..."       # vCenter hostname/IP
      username: "..."   # Admin username
      password: !vault  # Encrypted password
      validate_certs: false  # Required: SSL cert validation
      datacenters:
        - name: "..."   # Datacenter name
          clusters:
            - name: "..."      # Cluster name
              datastore: "..." # Datastore name
              vm_names: [...]  # VMs to deploy here

Alternative: Standalone ESXi

hypervisor:
  standalone_esxi:
    - name: "esxi-01"
      host: "esxi-01.lab.local"
      username: "root"
      password: !vault |...
      datastore: "datastore1"
      vm_names: ["tbac_vm01"]

docs.title: Images Section
docs.text:
Centralized catalog of OVA/ISO files:

images:
  - name: "tbac-base-ubuntu"           # Reference name
    type: "ova"                        # or "iso"
    version: "1.0.0"                   # Version tracking
    artifact:
      location: "artifactory"          # or "local"
      path: "NetworkServices/TBAC/tbac_2404-1.ova"
      url: "https://artifactory.devhub-cloud.cisco.com/artifactory/cx-svs-cdn-generic"
      checksum_sha256: "30d7063a685ddc11fe1b3e92c904bee6103a609ed1c6bc5431b8bd6fe1d04fb7"
      auth:
        type: "bearer_token"
        token: "eyJ2ZXIiOiIyIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIiwiYWxnIjoiUlMyNTYiLCJraWQiOiJicC1uNDZ5dnpGVGdYZmVSR0JkVUgxaW1WMTBrZnplT3lrZ1BQRmItVVhJIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJqZmFjQDAxZzNoOThtOTUzMmZ5MHpuMG5neDUxMDczL3VzZXJzL3N2c2F1dG8uZ2VuIiwic2NwIjoiYXBwbGllZC1wZXJtaXNzaW9ucy91c2VyIiwiYXVkIjoiKkAqIiwiaXNzIjoiamZhY0AwMWczaDk4bTk1MzJmeTB6bjBuZ3g1MTA3MyIsImV4cCI6MTc4Mjk5NjEwOCwiaWF0IjoxNzc1MjIwMTA4LCJqdGkiOiJmMmYxMmMyZi0yMjZiLTQ0MjYtOWY2Ny02NWY1ODZhNWE3ZWQifQ.MbgaLZM-KdvZn5Vmg9Hnxdv77DhjL2oOryBGDQ1kgryDIvR0ScsXB6nkJzuScGkY1P4x7yeh98nu7woNLUDEh0GLzOwHGJJI-paONvjMcp_yPjFDSSolwKocYj8E0CtPynkbIu0FRunwjtcjxdeKa0TpgTqx6SmyC5l2YV1OeIok7m1G7bi6QVPSvcTis0pdVzr7c4d2LzicRXaSX5I0nsqGTDLtpNxpYABaEdP7XtL334CXQe4Wggqcv0h-wMVQhahA4qmp6QOiuhJ9OBjujPt7NBZUxOOlK1DfGW76UUJI6vnudhWZA9zH6f0MsBuO3ORxMNUOAFkOPyl4LrL20A"

Supported Locations:

  • local: File on Ansible control node
  • artifactory: Artifactory repository (requires auth)
  • remote: SCP/SFTP from a remote server (requires sshpass on the control node) -- configure via the remote block instead of artifact

docs.title: Compute Section
docs.text:
Defines virtual machines:

compute:
  vms:
    - name: "tbac_vm01"             # VM name
      deploy_from: "ova"          # Deployment method
      ova_image_name: "tbac-base-ubuntu"   # Image reference
      hardware:
        cpu: 2                    # vCPU count
        memory_mb: 4096           # Memory in MB
        disk_gb: 40               # Disk size in GB
      network:
        interfaces:
          - name: "ens192"        # Interface name
            portgroup: "..."      # vSphere port group
            ip: "..."             # Static IP (optional)
            netmask: "..."        # Subnet mask
            gateway: "..."        # Default gateway
      os_config:
        hostname: "tbac_vm01"     # System hostname
        dns: ["8.8.8.8"]          # DNS servers
        users:                    # OS users
          - username: "admin"
            sudo: true

docs.title: Network Services (Optional)
docs.text:
Add network services (DNS, DHCP, NTP, TFTP, HTTP, LDAP, TACACS+, SNMP/LibreNMS):

network_services:
  dns:
    image_name: "mvance/unbound"        # Default from defaults/
    image_version: "1.21.1"             # Default from defaults/
    upstream_servers: ["8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4"]
    a_records:
      - name: "test.local."
        type: "A"
        value: "192.168.1.100"

Using defaults

If you use the defaults/defaults.tbac.yaml file, image_name and image_version are filled in automatically for all services. You only need to specify service-specific fields.

Learn more →

docs.title: Multi-File Configuration
docs.text: For better organization, split your configuration:

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
data/
├── hypervisor.tbac.yaml    # Infrastructure
├── services.tbac.yaml      # Network services
├── images.tbac.yaml        # Image catalog
└── vms/
    ├── web-servers.tbac.yaml # Web tier
    └── db-servers.tbac.yaml  # Database tier

defaults/
├── hypervisor.tbac.yaml    # Shared credentials
└── vms.tbac.yaml           # Default VM settings

docs.title: Example: Defaults File
docs.text:
defaults/vms.tbac.yaml:

defaults:
  testbed:
    compute:
      vms:
        hardware:
          cpu: 2
          memory_mb: 4096
          disk_gb: 50
        network:
          interfaces:
            - name: "ens192"
              portgroup: "VM Network"

docs.title: Example: Data File
docs.text:
data/vms/web.tbac.yaml:

testbed:
  compute:
    vms:
      - name: "tbac_vm01"
        deploy_from: "ova"
        ova_image_name: "tbac-base-ubuntu"
        hardware:
          disk_gb: 100     # Overrides default 50GB
        network:
          interfaces:
            - ip: "192.168.1.10"
              netmask: "255.255.255.0"
              gateway: "192.168.1.1"

Result: The merge system combines them automatically!

Merge System Details →

docs.title: Validating Your Configuration
docs.text:
Before deployment, always run the 4-tier validation pipeline:

# Quick validation (schema only)
python scripts/validate_schema.py

# Complete validation (schema + semantic + connectivity + IP uniqueness)
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags validate

Common validation errors and fixes:

Vault Password File Not Found

Error: 

[ERROR]: The vault password file /path/to/.vault_pass was not found

Cause: ansible.cfg requires .vault_pass to exist regardless of whether you use Ansible Vault.

Fix: Create the file in the project root. If you are not using Ansible Vault, an empty file is sufficient: 

touch .vault_pass
chmod 600 .vault_pass
If you are using Ansible Vault, populate it with your vault password instead.

Schema Validation Failed

Error: Required field missing: hypervisor.vcenter[0].datacenters

Fix: Add the datacenters list under your vCenter configuration

Network Service Image Fields Missing

Error: 

testbed.network_services.dns.image_version: Required field missing
testbed.network_services.dhcp.image_version: Required field missing
testbed.network_services.ntp.image_version: Required field missing

Fix: Add image_name and image_version to each network service. Use the defaults from defaults/defaults.tbac.yaml:

Service image_name image_version dns mvance/unbound 1.21.1 dhcp kosdk/dhcpd 4.4.1-2.3 ntp cturra/ntp latest tftp pghalliday/tftp latest http caddy alpine ldap bitnami/openldap 2.6 tacacs christianbecker/tac_plus-ng latest snmp librenms/librenms 26.2.0

Or point TBaC to defaults/defaults.tbac.yaml to have these applied automatically.

IP Address Invalid

Error: Invalid IP address format

Fix: Use proper IPv4 format: 192.168.1.10 (not 192.168.1.10/24)

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
Use descriptive, consistent names:

# Good
name: "tbac_vm01"
name: "tbac_vm02"

# Avoid
name: "vm1"
name: "test"

docs.title: Organization
docs.text:
1. Group by function: Separate web, app, and database VMs
  2. Use defaults: Define common values once
  3. Version control: Commit all configuration changes
  4. Document: Add comments for complex setups

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
1. Always encrypt passwords with Ansible Vault
  2. Never commit .vault_pass file
  3. Use strong passwords for all accounts
  4. Rotate credentials regularly

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that you have a configuration:

  1. Validate: Check configuration correctness
  2. Deploy: Provision your testbed
  3. Examples: Explore more complex setups

docs.title: Installation Guide
docs.text: Detailed installation reference for Testbed as Code. For a streamlined end-to-end walkthrough (clone → configure → deploy), see the Quick Start Guide.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This page covers:

  1. Automated Setup (recommended) — what the setup scripts do under the hood
  2. Manual Setup — step-by-step instructions when the scripts don't fit your environment

docs.title: Automated Setup
docs.text: The Quick Start Guide walks through the automated setup end-to-end. Below is a summary of what each script does.

docs.title: UV Setup Script (setup_uv.sh) — Recommended
docs.text:
• ✅ Installs UV if not present
  • ✅ Configures your shell (bash/zsh)
  • ✅ Installs Python 3.12
  • ✅ Creates virtual environment
  • ✅ Installs all Python dependencies
  • ✅ Installs Ansible collections

docs.title: pyenv Setup Script (setup.sh) — Traditional
docs.text:
• ✅ Detects OS and shell
  • ✅ Installs pyenv if not present
  • ✅ Configures shell automatically
  • ✅ Installs Python 3.12
  • ✅ Creates virtual environment
  • ✅ Installs all dependencies
  • ✅ Sets up auto-activation

After either script completes, restart your terminal:

source ~/.bashrc    # or ~/.bash_profile or ~/.zshrc

docs.title: Manual Setup
docs.text: If you prefer manual control or the automated scripts don't work:

docs.title: Step 1: Install UV or pyenv
docs.text:
UV (Modern)pyenv (Traditional) 
# Install UV
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# Add to PATH
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.cargo/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

# Verify installation
uv --version

# Install pyenv
curl -fsSL https://pyenv.run | bash

# Add to shell configuration
echo 'export PYENV_ROOT="$HOME/.pyenv"' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'command -v pyenv >/dev/null || export PATH="$PYENV_ROOT/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'eval "$(pyenv init -)"' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

# Verify installation
pyenv --version

docs.title: Step 2: Install Python 3.12
docs.text:
UVpyenv 
uv python install 3.12
uv python pin 3.12

pyenv install 3.12
pyenv local 3.12

docs.title: Step 3: Create Virtual Environment
docs.text:
UVpyenv 
# Create venv
uv venv --python 3.12

# Activate (optional with UV)
source .venv/bin/activate

# Create virtual environment
pyenv virtualenv 3.12 testbed-as-code

# Set local environment (auto-activates)
pyenv local testbed-as-code

docs.title: Step 4: Install Dependencies
docs.text:
UVTraditional pip 
# Install Python packages
uv pip install -r requirements.txt

# Install Ansible collections
uv run ansible-galaxy collection install -r collections/requirements.yml \
    -p collections/ansible_collections/

# Install Python packages
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Install Ansible collections
ansible-galaxy collection install -r collections/requirements.yml \
    -p collections/ansible_collections/

docs.title: Step 5: Verify Installation
docs.text:
# Check Python version
python --version  # Should be 3.12+

# Check Ansible version
ansible --version  # Should be 2.19+

# Verify collections
ansible-galaxy collection list

docs.title: Vault Password File
docs.text:
ansible.cfg requires a .vault_pass file to exist. See Quick Start — Step 5 for setup instructions.

If you are not using Ansible Vault, an empty file is sufficient:

touch .vault_pass
chmod 600 .vault_pass

Common error without this file

[ERROR]: The vault password file /path/to/.vault_pass was not found
Creating the file above resolves this error.

docs.title: Configure Ansible
docs.text:
The project includes a pre-configured ansible.cfg. Verify key settings:

[defaults]
vault_password_file = .vault_pass
action_plugins = ./plugins/action
host_key_checking = False
retry_files_enabled = False
log_path = ./ansible.log

docs.title: Check Installation
docs.text:
# Display installed versions
python --version
ansible --version
ansible-galaxy collection list

Expected output: 

Python 3.12.x

ansible [core 2.19.2]

ansible.posix                            2.1.0  
ansible.utils                            6.0.2  
community.docker                         5.2.0  
community.general                        12.6.0 
community.library_inventory_filtering_v1 1.1.5  
community.vmware                         6.2.0  
vmware.vmware                            2.8.0  
vmware.vmware_rest                       4.10.0

docs.title: Python Version Mismatch
docs.text:
Error: Python 3.12+ required

Solution: 

# Verify Python version
python --version

# If wrong version, reinstall with UV/pyenv
uv python install 3.12

docs.title: Ansible Collection Install Fails
docs.text:
Error: Collection install failed

Solution: 

# Clean and reinstall
rm -rf collections/ansible_collections/
ansible-galaxy collection install -r collections/requirements.yml \
    -p collections/ansible_collections/ --force

docs.title: Vault Password File Not Found
docs.text:
Error: 

[ERROR]: The vault password file /path/to/.vault_pass was not found

Cause: ansible.cfg requires .vault_pass to exist. The file is missing from your project root.

Solution: Create the file. If you are not using Ansible Vault, an empty file is sufficient: 

touch .vault_pass
chmod 600 .vault_pass

If you are using Ansible Vault, populate the file with your vault password instead: 

echo 'your_vault_password' > .vault_pass
chmod 600 .vault_pass

docs.title: Permission Denied
docs.text:
Error: Permission denied: .vault_pass

Solution: 

chmod 600 .vault_pass

docs.title: Module Not Found
docs.text:
Error: No module named 'pyvmomi'

Solution: 

# Verify virtual environment is active
which python

# Reinstall dependencies
uv pip install -r requirements.txt --force-reinstall

docs.title: Upgrade to Latest Version
docs.text:
# Pull latest code
git pull origin develop

# Update dependencies
uv pip install -r requirements.txt --upgrade

# Update Ansible collections
ansible-galaxy collection install -r collections/requirements.yml \
    -p collections/ansible_collections/ --force

docs.title: Migration Between Versions
docs.text: See Changelog for breaking changes and migration guides.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Before installing Testbed as Code, ensure your environment meets these requirements.

docs.title: Operating System
docs.text:
Supported Platforms

  • macOS: Fully supported
  • Linux: Fully supported (Ubuntu, RHEL, CentOS, etc.)
  • Windows: ❌ Not currently supported

docs.title: Python Version
docs.text:
• Python 3.12 or higher is required
  • We provide two setup scripts that handle Python, Ansible, and all toolchain dependencies automatically:
    • setup_uv.sh — installs UV and uses it to manage Python and dependencies (recommended)
    • setup.sh — installs pyenv and uses it to manage Python and dependencies (traditional)
  • If you prefer to manage Python yourself, we recommend UV (modern, fast) or pyenv (traditional)
UV (Recommended)pyenv (Traditional) 
# Check if UV is installed
uv --version

# Install UV if needed
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# Install Python 3.12
uv python install 3.12

Benefits: 10-100x faster than pip, built-in Python management

# Check if pyenv is installed
pyenv --version

# Install Python 3.12
pyenv install 3.12
pyenv local 3.12

Benefits: Mature, widely used, auto-activation support

docs.title: VMware Infrastructure
docs.text: You need access to one of the following:

docs.title: Option 1: vCenter Server
docs.text:
• vCenter Server 6.7 or higher
  • Administrative credentials for vCenter
  • Network connectivity from Ansible control node to vCenter API (HTTPS/443)
  • At least one ESXi host managed by vCenter
  • Datastore with sufficient space for VM deployment
  • Internet access required for downloading OVA images during deployment

docs.title: Option 2: Standalone ESXi
docs.text:
• ESXi 6.7 or higher
  • Root or administrative credentials
  • Network connectivity from Ansible control node to ESXi (SSH/22, HTTPS/443)
  • Datastore with sufficient space
  • Internet access required for downloading OVA images during deployment

docs.title: Required Resources
docs.text:
Minimum resources needed on your VMware infrastructure:

Resource Minimum Recommended CPU 2 cores per VM 4 cores per VM Memory 4 GB per VM 8 GB per VM Disk 50 GB per VM 150 GB per VM

docs.title: Connectivity
docs.text:
• Management Network: Ansible control node must reach vCenter/ESXi management interface
  • VM Network: VMs need network access based on your configuration
  • Internet Access: Required for downloading OVA files during deployment (direct or via proxy configuration)

docs.title: Ports
docs.text: Service Port Purpose vCenter HTTPS 443 vCenter API access ESXi SSH 22 Direct ESXi management ESXi HTTPS 443 ESXi API access

docs.title: Testbed as Code VM Images
docs.text:
• Testbed as Code uses a specific OVA file based on the Ubuntu 24.04 Cloud Image with additional packages installed to support Docker containers and all packages required for automatic provisioning of settings using cloud-init.
  • This OVA will automatically be downloaded from Artifactory when executing the deploy role from the Ansible playbook.

docs.title: Credentials Needed
docs.text:
Gather the following information before starting:

Item Description Example vCenter Host FQDN or IP address vcenter.lab.local vCenter Username Admin user administrator@vsphere.local vCenter Password Admin password (can be encrypted) Datacenter Name vCenter datacenter Lab-DC Cluster Name vCenter cluster Cluster1 Datastore Name Storage location shared-storage

Security Best Practice

Never store passwords in plain text. We'll use Ansible Vault to encrypt all credentials during setup.

docs.title: Required
docs.text:
• Git for version control
  • SSH client for VM access
  • IDE client (VSCode, Windsurf, etc.)

docs.title: Required Knowledge
docs.text:
• Basic understanding of YAML syntax
  • Familiarity with VMware concepts (vCenter, ESXi, VMs, datastores)
  • Basic Ansible concepts

docs.title: Verification Checklist
docs.text:
Before proceeding to installation, verify you have:

  • macOS or Linux system
  • Access to vCenter or ESXi with admin credentials
  • Network connectivity to VMware infrastructure with Internet access
  • Sufficient resources for VM deployment
  • Git and SSH client installed

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Once you've verified all prerequisites, continue to Installation to set up Testbed as Code.

Need Help?

If you're missing any prerequisites:

  • Python Setup: See our Installation Guide for detailed Python setup
  • VMware Access: Contact your infrastructure team for access

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: This page walks through the complete lifecycle of a Testbed as Code deployment — from cloning the repository to a fully running testbed with network services. For manual installation options and troubleshooting, see the Installation Guide.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Clone Repository] --> B[Run Setup Script]
    B --> C[Configure Credentials]
    C --> D[Define Testbed]
    D --> E[Validate Configuration]
    E --> F{Validation Passed?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Deploy VMs]
    G --> H[Deploy Network Services]
    H --> I[Verify Testbed]

    style A fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#ff9000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#00a86b,color:#fff

docs.title: Step 1: Clone the Repository
docs.text: The repository uses SSH for cloning. If you don't already have an SSH key set up, follow the steps below before cloning.

docs.title: Step 1a: Generate an SSH Key (if needed)
docs.text:
Check whether you already have a key:

ls ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

If the file doesn't exist, generate a new key:

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your_cec@cisco.com"

Accept the default location (~/.ssh/id_ed25519) and set a passphrase when prompted. Your keys will be stored at:

File Purpose ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 Private key — keep this secret, never share it ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub Public key — this is what you add to GitHub

docs.title: Step 1b: Add the Public Key to GitHub
docs.text:
Copy the public key to your clipboard:

macOSLinux 
pbcopy < ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
# Then manually copy the output

Then add it to GitHub (login using your cec_cisco as the username which will prompt SSO login):

  1. Go to GitHub → Settings → SSH and GPG keys
  2. Click New SSH key
  3. Give it a descriptive title (e.g. My MacBook)
  4. Paste the public key into the Key field
  5. Click Add SSH key

docs.title: Step 1c: Verify the SSH Connection
docs.text:
ssh -T git@github.com

You should see:

Hi <cec_cisco>! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.

docs.title: Step 1d: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone git@github.com:cxsvs-int/svs-testbed-as-code.git
cd svs-testbed-as-code

docs.title: Step 2: Run the Setup Script
docs.text:
Choose the setup script that matches your preferred Python toolchain:

UV (Recommended)pyenv (Traditional) 
# Run automated setup
chmod +x setup_uv.sh
./setup_uv.sh

Installs UV, Python 3.12, Ansible, and all required dependencies.

# Run automated setup
chmod +x setup.sh
./setup.sh

Installs pyenv, Python 3.12, Ansible, and all required dependencies.

After the script completes, continue to the next step to activate the environment.

docs.title: Step 3: Activate the Virtual Environment
docs.text:
The setup scripts create a Python virtual environment in the repository root. Activate it based on which setup script you ran:

UV (Recommended)pyenv (Traditional) 
source .venv/bin/activate

UV creates the virtual environment at .venv/ in the repository root.

source .venv/bin/activate

pyenv creates the virtual environment at .venv/ in the repository root. Ensure pyenv has set the correct Python version first:

pyenv local 3.12
source .venv/bin/activate

Verify the environment is active:

ansible --version
python --version

Tip

You will need to activate the environment each time you open a new terminal session.

docs.title: Step 4: Define Your Testbed
docs.text:
The examples/ directory contains ready-made configurations for the three supported environments. Pick the one that best matches your infrastructure and use it as your starting point:

File Use when standalone-esxi.tbac.yaml Connecting directly to a single ESXi host vcenter-cluster.tbac.yaml vCenter managing one or more clusters vcenter-non-cluster.tbac.yaml vCenter with standalone ESXi hosts (no cluster objects)

Copy the appropriate example into data/:

cp examples/vcenter-cluster.tbac.yaml data/testbed.tbac.yaml

Then open data/testbed.tbac.yaml and update the values for your environment. At minimum, configure:

  • hypervisor — your vCenter or ESXi connection details
  • compute — the VMs you want to deploy
  • network_services (optional) — DNS, DHCP, NTP, and other services

These are the only files you need to worry about — everything else in the repository is managed for you:

testbed-as-code/
├── data/
│   └── testbed.tbac.yaml       # Your environment configuration (copy from examples/)
├── defaults/
│   └── defaults.tbac.yaml      # Default values (provided by the repo, use caution when editing)
└── playbooks/
    └── tbac.yaml               # Main playbook entrypoint

See the Configuration Guide and Examples for a full field-by-field reference.

Minimal inline configuration example

If you prefer to start from scratch instead of copying an example file, here is a minimal configuration:

testbed:
  name: "quick-start-lab"
  type: "shared"

  hypervisor:
    vcenter:
      - host: "vcenter.lab.local"
        username: "administrator@vsphere.local"
        password: !vault |
          $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256
          ...your encrypted password...
        validate_certs: false
        datacenters:
          - name: "Lab-DC"
            clusters:
              - name: "Lab-Cluster"
                datastore: "shared-storage"
                vm_names: ["tbac_vm01"]

  compute:
    vms:
      - name: "tbac_vm01"
        deploy_from: "ova"
        ova_image_name: "tbac-base-ubuntu"
        hardware:
          cpu: 2
          memory_mb: 4096
          disk_gb: 40
        network:
          interfaces:
            - name: "ens192"
              portgroup: "VM Network"
              ip: "192.168.1.100"
              netmask: "255.255.255.0"
              gateway: "192.168.1.1"
        os_config:
          hostname: "tbac_vm01"
          dns: ["8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4"]

docs.title: Step 5: Configure Credentials
docs.text:
Create a vault password file and encrypt your vCenter or ESXi credentials:

# Create vault password file
echo 'your_secure_vault_password' > .vault_pass
chmod 600 .vault_pass

# Encrypt your vCenter password
ansible-vault encrypt_string 'your_vcenter_password' --name 'password'

Copy the encrypted output and replace the password field in data/testbed.tbac.yaml with it.

Security

Never commit .vault_pass or any file containing plaintext credentials.

docs.title: Step 6: Validate Configuration
docs.text:
Run validation before deploying to catch configuration errors early:

ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags validate

This runs the 4-tier validation pipeline (schema, semantic, connectivity, and IP uniqueness checks). Fix any reported errors and re-run until all stages pass.

docs.title: Step 7: Deploy
docs.text:
Run the full playbook to merge configuration, validate, deploy VMs, and configure network services:

ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml

The playbook executes the role pipeline in order:

Role What it does common Merges defaults/ + data/ → .tbac/current.tbac.yaml validate Runs all four validation stages deploy Provisions VMs (OVA download + clone) and deploys services operate Performs day-2 operations (LDAP sync, password resets)

To deploy only VMs without network services:

ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy_vm

To deploy only network services on already-running VMs:

ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy_services

docs.title: Step 8: Verify the Testbed
docs.text:
Once deployment completes, verify everything is running:

# SSH into a deployed VM
ssh ubuntu@<vm_ip>

# Check the generated pyATS testbed file
cat out/pyats_testbed.yaml

Check that Docker services are running on the service VM:

ssh ubuntu@<service_vm_ip> "docker ps"

docs.title: Re-running and Idempotency
docs.text:
The full playbook is idempotent — re-running it will not recreate VMs or services that already exist. Use tags to target specific phases when iterating:

# Re-validate only
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags validate

# Redeploy services only
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy_services

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
VM Deployment Failed

Check vCenter connectivity and credentials: 

ansible-playbook playbooks/test_vcenter.yaml

Schema Validation Errors

Review your YAML syntax and required fields: 

python scripts/validate_schema.py

OVA Not Found

Verify the OVA path is correct and accessible: 

ls -lh /path/to/your-ova-file.ova

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Check the logs: cat ansible.log
  • Run with verbose output: ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml -v
  • Review the FAQ: Common Questions
  • Report issues: GitHub Issues

docs.title: What's Next?
docs.text: Now that you have a running testbed, explore more advanced features:

docs.title: Add Network Services
docs.text:
Add DNS, DHCP, NTP, TFTP, HTTP, LDAP, TACACS+, or SNMP/LibreNMS to your testbed:

network_services:
  dns:
    image_name: "mvance/unbound"
    image_version: "1.21.1"
    upstream_servers: ["8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4"]
    a_records:
      - name: "test.local."
        type: "A"
        value: "192.168.1.100"

Learn more about Network Services →

docs.title: Deploy Multiple VMs
docs.text:
Scale up to a multi-VM environment:

compute:
  vms:
    - name: "tbac_vm01"
      # ... config ...
    - name: "tbac_vm02"
      # ... config ...
    - name: "tbac_vm03"
      # ... config ...

See Multi-VM Examples →

docs.title: Use the Merge System
docs.text:
Organize your configuration with defaults and data files:

defaults/
  ├── hypervisor.tbac.yaml # Shared credentials
  └── vms.tbac.yaml        # Default VM settings

data/
  ├── hypervisor.tbac.yaml # Environment-specific
  └── vms/
      ├── web.tbac.yaml
      └── db.tbac.yaml

Learn about Configuration Merge →

# Default paths

python scripts/validate_schema.py \
  -s schema/schema.yaml \

Options:

# Quick validation
python scripts/validate_schema.py

# Complete validation (schema + semantic + connectivity + IP uniqueness)

docs.title: Configuration Merge System
docs.text: Understanding how defaults and data files are intelligently combined.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The merge system allows you to define reusable defaults separately from environment-specific data, with intelligent merging logic.

docs.title: Merge Architecture
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[defaults/*.tbac.yaml] --> D[Merge Defaults]
    B[data/**/*.tbac.yaml] --> E[Merge Data]

    D --> F[merged_defaults.yaml]
    E --> G[merged_data.yaml]

    F --> H[Final Merge]
    G --> H

    H --> I[testbed.tbac.yaml]

    style D fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#00c853,color:#fff

docs.title: Dictionary Merge
docs.text:
Data overrides defaults:

# defaults/vms.tbac.yaml
hardware:
  cpu: 2
  memory_mb: 4096

# data/vms/web.tbac.yaml
hardware:
  disk_gb: 100

# Result
hardware:
  cpu: 2              # From defaults
  memory_mb: 4096     # From defaults
  disk_gb: 100        # From data

docs.title: List with name Key (Deduplicated)
docs.text:
# defaults
interfaces:
  - name: eth0
    network: Default

# data
interfaces:
  - name: eth0       # Overrides
    ip: 10.0.0.1
  - name: eth1       # Appends

# Result: 2 interfaces (eth0 updated, eth1 added)

docs.title: Simple Lists (Appended)
docs.text:
# defaults
dns: ["8.8.8.8"]

# data
dns: ["8.8.4.4"]

# Result
dns: ["8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4"]

docs.title: Merge Precedence
docs.text:
Values are resolved in this order (highest wins):

  1. data/ — environment-specific overrides
  2. defaults/ — shared baseline values
  3. Role defaults — built-in Ansible role defaults (should be empty per project convention)

docs.title: Vault-Encrypted Values
docs.text: Ansible Vault-encrypted values (!vault tags) are preserved through the merge pipeline. The merge plugins detect vault-tagged strings and pass them through without attempting to parse or merge their contents.

docs.title: Custom Merge Directories
docs.text:
# Use different directories
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml \
  -e "defaults_dir=defaults-prod" \
  -e "data_dir=data-prod"

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Put shared values in defaults/
  2. Put environment-specific values in data/
  3. Organize data files by function
  4. Use meaningful file names
  5. Organize defaults and data files consistently across environments

docs.title: pyATS Integration
docs.text: Automatic generation of pyATS testbed files for test automation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Testbed as Code automatically generates pyATS-compatible testbed files from your configuration, enabling integration with Cisco pyATS testing framework.

docs.title: Location
docs.text:
By default, the file is generated at:

<project_root>/out/pyats_testbed.yaml

The output location is configurable via testbed.pyats.output_directory.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
testbed:
  name: testbed_as_code

devices:
  VCENTER_LAB_LOCAL:
    type: server
    os: generic
    credentials:
      default:
        username: administrator@vsphere.local
        password: "***"
    connections:
      defaults:
        class: unicon.Unicon
      https:
        protocol: https
        ip: vcenter.lab.local
        port: 443

  WEB_SERVER_01:
    type: server
    os: linux
    credentials:
      default:
        username: tbac
        password: "***"
    connections:
      defaults:
        class: unicon.Unicon
      ssh:
        protocol: ssh
        ip: 192.168.1.10
        port: 22

docs.title: Load Testbed
docs.text:
from pyats.topology import loader

testbed = loader.load('./out/pyats_testbed.yaml')

docs.title: Connect to Devices
docs.text:
# Connect to a VM
device = testbed.devices['WEB_SERVER_01']
device.connect()

# Execute commands
output = device.execute('show version')

docs.title: Enabling pyATS File Generation
docs.text:
pyATS testbed file rendering is disabled by default. To enable it, set the create_testbed_file toggle in your data model:

testbed:
  pyats:
    create_testbed_file: true

When create_testbed_file is false or absent, the common role skips rendering entirely — no testbed file is created. This is the recommended default for environments where pyATS integration is not required, as it prevents unnecessary file generation and avoids CI pipeline failures in those environments.

Field Type Required Default Description pyats.create_testbed_file bool No false Set to true to generate pyats_testbed.yaml pyats.output_directory string No <project_root>/out Absolute path where the pyATS testbed file is written. Defaults to the out/ directory at the project root.

Custom output location

To write the pyATS testbed outside the project directory, set output_directory to an absolute path: 

testbed:
  pyats:
    create_testbed_file: true
    output_directory: "/opt/pyats/testbeds"

Warning

Always use an absolute path when overriding output_directory. Relative paths resolve from the shell's working directory, not the project root, which can produce unexpected results.

docs.title: Generation
docs.text:
When create_testbed_file: true is set, the testbed file is generated by the common role:

# Generate testbed file (requires testbed.pyats.create_testbed_file: true)
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags render_pyats

docs.title: Role Reference
docs.text: Detailed documentation for all Ansible roles.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Testbed as Code uses five primary roles following a strict execution order:

common → validate → deploy → operate → remove (roadmap)
  • common: Configuration merge and pyATS generation
  • validate: Multi-tier validation
  • deploy: Infrastructure provisioning
  • operate: Runtime operations (LDAP password resets, user/group sync)
  • remove: Safe cleanup operations

docs.title: common Role
docs.text:
Purpose: Configuration management and testbed generation

Tasks:

  • Merge defaults and data files
  • Generate final testbed configuration
  • Render pyATS testbed file

Variables:

Variable Default Description defaults_dir defaults Defaults source directory data_dir data Data source directory output_dir roles/common/files Output directory

Outputs:

  • roles/common/files/merged_defaults.yaml
  • roles/common/files/merged_data.yaml
  • .tbac/current.tbac.yaml (merged testbed configuration)
  • out/pyats_testbed.yaml (when pyats.create_testbed_file: true)

docs.title: validate Role
docs.text:
Purpose: Multi-tier validation

Dependencies: common (explicit via include_role in tasks/main.yaml)

Tasks:

  • Schema validation (YAMALE) — validate_schema.yaml
  • Semantic validation (Python rules across 6 categories) — validate_semantics.yaml
  • Connectivity testing (vCenter/ESXi) — validate_connectivity.yaml
  • Network IP availability check (ping + ARP) — validate_ip_uniqueness.yaml
  • Model file management and change tracking (.tbac/ directory)

Variables:

Variable Default Description schema_path schema/schema.yaml Schema file path force_run_all false Force model cleanup skip_ip_validation (undefined — validation runs) Set to true to skip Tier 4 IP uniqueness check

Tags:

Tag Scope validate Run all validation tiers ip_validation Run only IP uniqueness check (validate_ip_uniqueness.yaml) network_validation Alias for ip_validation

Semantic Validation Rules (29 total):

Category Rules testbed/ (1) 101_pyats_toggle hypervisors/ (5) 102_hostname_protocol_prefix, 200_vm_references, 201_vcenter_config, 500_infrastructure_config, 600_host_uniqueness vms/ (4) 100_vm_name_uniqueness, 150_vm_proxy_config, 400_vm_deployment_conflicts, 700_ip_address_conflicts network_services/ (16) 300_service_references, 301_tftp_path_validation, 302_docker_image_validation, 303_snmp_validation, 304_port_conflict_detection, 305_dhcp_subnet_overlap, 960_ldap_service_required, 961_ldap_base_dn_format, 962_ldap_admin_password_required, 970_tacacs_service_required, 971_tacacs_host_secret_required, 972_tacacs_user_name_uniqueness, 973_tacacs_host_name_uniqueness, 974_tacacs_profile_name_uniqueness, 975_tacacs_spawnd_ordering, 976_tacacs_user_member_profile_reference images/ (2) 800_image_artifact_auth, 801_image_remote_source application_services/ (1) 900_netbox_secret_key_length

docs.title: deploy Role
docs.text:
Purpose: Infrastructure provisioning

Dependencies: validate (explicit via include_role in tasks/main.yaml)

Tasks:

  • VM deployment (template-based or OVA-based)
  • Proxy configuration (/etc/environment, APT proxy) — applied when os_config.proxy is defined
  • Network service deployment (DNS, DHCP, NTP, TFTP, HTTP, LDAP, TACACS+, SNMP/LibreNMS)
  • Application service deployment (Netbox)
  • Docker installation and engine configuration
  • Cloud-init and guest customization

Tags:

Tag Scope deploy Run full deployment deploy_vm VM provisioning only deploy_proxy Proxy configuration only (deploy_proxy_config.yaml) deploy_base Base OS configuration (includes proxy) deploy_services Network service deployment only deploy_ldap LDAP service deployment only

docs.title: operate Role
docs.text:
Purpose: Runtime operations on deployed infrastructure

Dependencies: deploy (explicit via include_role in tasks/main.yaml)

Tasks:

  • LDAP password resets for seed users
  • LDAP user synchronization (add/update users from data model)
  • LDAP group synchronization (add/update groups from data model)

Task Organization:

roles/operate/tasks/
├── main.yaml
└── service_operations/
    └── ldap/
        ├── main.yaml
        ├── _per_vm.yaml
        ├── reset_password.yaml
        ├── sync_users.yaml
        └── sync_groups.yaml

Tags:

Tag Scope operate Run all operate tasks operate_ldap All LDAP operations (password reset + user sync + group sync) operate_ldap_sync_users LDAP user synchronization only operate_ldap_sync_groups LDAP group synchronization only operate_ldap_reset_password LDAP password reset for seed users only

Running:

# Run all LDAP operations
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags operate_ldap

# Run specific LDAP operations
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags operate_ldap_sync_users
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags operate_ldap_sync_groups
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags operate_ldap_reset_password

docs.title: remove Role
docs.text:
Purpose: Safe cleanup (in roadmap)

Dependencies: None

Safety Flags:

  • vm_delete_mode: false
  • network_service_delete_mode: false
  • infrastructure_delete_mode: false

docs.title: Running Roles
docs.text:
# Running validate executes common → validate
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags validate

# Running deploy executes common → validate → deploy
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy

# Running operate executes common → validate → deploy → operate
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags operate

# Running merge executes only common
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags merge

docs.title: Schema Reference
docs.text: Complete schema reference for Testbed as Code, covering both the YAMALE runtime schema and the JSON Schema for IDE support.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
TBaC uses two schema files that mirror the same data model for different purposes:

File Format Purpose schema/schema.yaml YAMALE Runtime validation via Ansible (testbed_schema_validate plugin) schema/schema.json JSON Schema Draft 7 IDE-time assistance — autocompletion, hover docs, inline validation

Both files must be kept in sync when adding new fields to the data model.

docs.title: IDE Schema Support (VS Code)
docs.text: The JSON Schema (schema/schema.json) provides real-time assistance while authoring *.tbac.yaml files in VS Code. No Ansible run is required — feedback is instant as you type.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text: Feature Description Autocompletion Press Ctrl+Space inside any mapping to see all valid property names Hover documentation Hover over any key to see its description and type information Inline validation Missing required fields and wrong types are flagged immediately with squiggles Enum hints Fields like deploy_from and type show allowed values in the dropdown Required field enforcement Required fields that are absent are highlighted before you run Ansible

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Step 1 — Install the Red Hat YAML extension

Search for redhat.vscode-yaml in the VS Code Extensions panel (Cmd+Shift+X) and install it.

Step 2 — Confirm the schema association

The association is already configured in .vscode/settings.json in the repo:

{
  "yaml.schemas": {
    "./schema/schema.json": "**/*.tbac.yaml"
  }
}

No further configuration is needed.

Step 3 — Reload VS Code (first time only)

Open the Command Palette (Cmd+Shift+P) and run Developer: Reload Window.

docs.title: Verifying It Is Working
docs.text:
1. Open any *.tbac.yaml file — try data/testbed.tbac.yaml or any file under examples/

  2. What you should see immediately:

    • Hover over any key → tooltip shows the field description and type
    • Type a new key inside testbed: → autocomplete dropdown shows all valid properties
    • A required field that is missing → yellow/red squiggle with a validation message
    • An invalid value (wrong type, disallowed enum value) → inline error

  3. Check the status bar — at the bottom of the VS Code window, the right side shows the active schema name (e.g. tbac) when a matched file is open. If it shows no schema, the extension may need a reload.

Note

The JSON Schema is used solely for IDE assistance. Runtime validation continues to use the YAMALE schema via the testbed_schema_validate Ansible action plugin. When adding a new field, update both schema/schema.yaml and schema/schema.json.

docs.text: testbed: include('testbed', required=True)

docs.title: User Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the comprehensive Testbed as Code user guide. This section provides in-depth coverage of all features and workflows.

docs.title: What's in This Guide
docs.text: This user guide is organized into focused topics to help you understand and use Testbed as Code effectively:

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Understand the role-based design, dependencies, and architectural patterns that power Testbed as Code.

docs.title: Data Model
docs.text: Learn the structure and organization of testbed configurations, from hypervisors to applications.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text: Discover the complete deployment workflow from configuration merge to deployment.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text: Master the deployment process including VM provisioning and service configuration.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Understand the four-tier validation system and how to ensure configuration correctness.

docs.title: Infrastructure as Code
docs.text:
Testbed as Code follows Infrastructure as Code (IaC) principles:

  • Declarative: Describe the desired state, not the steps to achieve it
  • Version Controlled: All configurations are stored in Git
  • Repeatable: Deploy identical environments consistently
  • Testable: Validate configurations before deployment

docs.title: Network as Code Philosophy
docs.text:
Based on the proven Network as Code framework, Testbed as Code brings these principles to testbed management:

  • Data Models: Structured YAML definitions
  • Schema Validation: YAMALE-based validation
  • Semantic Rules: Python-based business logic validation
  • Idempotency: Safe to run multiple times
  • Modular Design: Role-based architecture

docs.title: Role-Based Architecture
docs.text:
Five specialized roles handle different aspects of testbed management:

graph TB
    A[common<br/>Configuration Merge] --> B[validate<br/>Validation & Checks]
    B --> C[deploy<br/>Deployment]
    C --> D[operate<br/>Runtime Operations]
    E[remove<br/>Cleanup]

    style A fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff9000,color:#fff

Role Purposes:

  • common: Merges defaults and data files, generates pyATS testbed
  • validate: Schema validation, semantic checks, connectivity tests, IP uniqueness probing
  • deploy: VM deployment, network services, application installation
  • operate: Day-2 runtime operations (LDAP password resets, user/group sync)
  • remove: Safe infrastructure cleanup with safety controls (planned)

docs.title: Development Workflow
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Edit Config] --> B[Validate]
    B --> C{Valid?}
    C -->|No| A
    C -->|Yes| D[Deploy]
    D --> E[Test]
    E --> F{Works?}
    F -->|No| A
    F -->|Yes| G[Commit]

    style A fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff9000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#00c853,color:#fff

docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Define Testbed] --> B[Peer Review]
    B --> C[Validate]
    C --> D[Test Deploy]
    D --> E[Review Results]
    E --> F[Deploy Production]
    F --> G[Monitor]

    style A fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ff9000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#00c853,color:#fff
    style G fill:#00c853,color:#fff

docs.title: Configuration Management
docs.text:
1. Use the Merge System: Separate defaults from environment-specific data
  2. Version Control: Commit all configuration changes
  3. Validate Early: Run validation before starting work
  4. Test Locally: Use dry-run mode before production deployment

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
1. Encrypt Credentials: Always use Ansible Vault
  2. Limit Access: Use role-based access control
  3. Audit Changes: Track all configuration modifications
  4. Rotate Credentials: Regularly update passwords

docs.title: Organization
docs.text:
1. Consistent Naming: Use clear, descriptive names for VMs and services
  2. Documentation: Comment complex configurations
  3. Modular Files: Split large configurations into logical files
  4. Tag Resources: Use metadata for organization

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Understanding the design principles and architecture of Testbed as Code.

docs.title: Design Philosophy
docs.text:
Testbed as Code follows proven patterns from the Network as Code framework, emphasizing:

  • Declarative Configuration: Describe what you want, not how to achieve it
  • Data-Driven Approach: YAML-based data models drive all operations
  • Idempotent Operations: Safe to run multiple times
  • Modular Design: Role-based architecture with clear responsibilities
  • Validation First: Multiple validation layers before deployment

docs.title: Role-Based Architecture
docs.text:
The system is built around five specialized Ansible roles following a strict execution order:

common → validate → deploy → operate → remove (roadmap)

docs.title: Architecture Diagram
docs.text:
graph TB
    subgraph "Core Roles"
        A[common<br/>Config Merge & pyATS]
        B[validate<br/>Schema & Semantic]
        C[deploy<br/>VM & Services]
        D[operate<br/>Runtime Operations]
    end

    subgraph "Utility Roles"
        E[remove<br/>Cleanup]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D

    style A fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff9000,color:#fff

docs.title: Role Summary
docs.text:
Role Purpose Dependencies Key Outputs common Configuration merge and pyATS generation None (runs first) .tbac/current.tbac.yaml, out/pyats_testbed.yaml validate 4-tier validation pipeline (schema, semantic, connectivity, IP) common Validation pass/fail, .tbac/changes.diff deploy VM provisioning (template/OVA) and service deployment validate Running VMs, Docker services operate Day-2 operations (LDAP sync, password resets) deploy Updated LDAP state remove Safe infrastructure cleanup (roadmap) None —

For detailed role documentation including variables, tags, task organization, and semantic validation rules, see the Role Reference.

docs.title: Dependency Chain
docs.text:
Ansible automatically resolves role dependencies:

graph LR
    A[common] --> B[validate]
    B --> C[deploy]
    C --> D[operate]

    A1[Merge Config] --> A2[Generate pyATS]
    B1[Schema Check] --> B2[Semantic Check] --> B3[Connectivity Test] --> B4[IP Uniqueness]
    C1[Deploy VMs] --> C2[Configure Services]
    D1[LDAP Ops]

    A --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    B --> B1
    C --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    D --> D1

    style A fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff

docs.title: How Dependencies Work
docs.text:
Dependencies use explicit include_role in each role's tasks/main.yaml with always tags:

  1. Explicit includes: Each role includes its dependency at the top of tasks/main.yaml
  2. Always tag: Dependencies tagged with always ensure they run regardless of tag selection
  3. Transitive resolution: deploy includes validate, which includes common
  4. Visible chain: Dependencies are visible in code, not hidden in meta files

docs.title: Running Roles with Tags
docs.text:
# Running validate executes common → validate
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags validate

# Running deploy executes common → validate → deploy
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy

# Running operate executes common → validate → deploy → operate
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags operate

# Running merge executes only common
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags merge

docs.title: Plugin Architecture
docs.text: Custom Ansible action plugins extend functionality:

docs.title: Action Plugins
docs.text:
Located in plugins/action/:

Plugin Purpose testbed_schema_validate YAMALE schema validation testbed_semantic_validate Python rules validation merge_defaults_files Merge defaults directory merge_data_files Merge data directory merge_testbed_files Final testbed merge

docs.title: Plugin Benefits
docs.text:
• Reusable Logic: Common code in plugins/action/common/
  • Consistent Interface: Standard Ansible module interface
  • Error Handling: Comprehensive error messages
  • Testing: Full unit test coverage

Plugin Development →

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text:
Understanding how data flows through the system:

graph TD
    A[defaults/*.tbac.yaml] --> D[merge_defaults_files]
    B[data/**/*.tbac.yaml] --> E[merge_data_files]

    D --> F[merged_defaults.yaml]
    E --> G[merged_data.yaml]

    F --> H[merge_testbed_files]
    G --> H

    H --> I[testbed.tbac.yaml]
    I --> J[validate role]
    I --> K[pyATS generator]

    J --> L[deploy role]
    K --> M[pyats_testbed.yaml]

    style A fill:#e8f4f8
    style B fill:#e8f4f8
    style D fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff

docs.title: Key Steps
docs.text:
1. Merge defaults: All files in defaults/ → merged_defaults.yaml
  2. Merge data: All files in data/ → merged_data.yaml
  3. Final merge: Defaults + Data → testbed.tbac.yaml
  4. Validate: Schema + semantic checks
  5. Deploy: Provision VMs and services
  6. Generate: Create pyATS testbed file

docs.title: Configuration Merge Logic
docs.text:
The merge system intelligently combines defaults/ and data/ files using dictionary deep-merge (data overrides defaults), named-list deduplication (items matched by name key), and simple-list appending. The data/ layer always takes precedence over defaults/.

Merge System Details →

docs.title: Credential Management
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Plain Password] --> B[Ansible Vault]
    B --> C[Encrypted in YAML]
    C --> D[Runtime Decryption]
    D --> E[VMware API]

    style A fill:#ff5252,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff9000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#00c853,color:#fff
    style D fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff

docs.title: Security Features
docs.text:
• Vault Encryption: All passwords encrypted with Ansible Vault
  • No Plain Text: Credentials never stored unencrypted
  • Runtime-Only: Passwords decrypted only during execution
  • Vault Preservation: Encrypted values maintain encryption through merge

docs.title: Safety Controls
docs.text:
Multiple layers prevent accidental destruction:

  1. Explicit Flags: Delete operations require explicit boolean flags
  2. Validation Gates: Must pass validation before deployment
  3. Change Tracking: .tbac/ directory tracks all changes
  4. Dry Run Mode: Test deployments without making changes

docs.title: Single Testbed
docs.text:
• VMs: Up to 50+ VMs per testbed
  • Services: Multiple DHCP/DNS/NTP services
  • Networks: Complex multi-network topologies

docs.title: Multi-Environment
docs.text:
• Shared defaults: Common configurations across environments
  • Environment data: Separate data directories (dev, staging, prod)
  • Parallel operations: Independent testbed deployments

docs.title: Enterprise Scale
docs.text:
• CI/CD Integration: Automated validation and deployment
  • Version Control: Git-based configuration management
  • Team Collaboration: Multiple contributors with merge system

docs.title: Extension Points
docs.text:
The architecture supports customization:

  • Custom Plugins: Add new action plugins in plugins/action/
  • Custom Roles: Create additional roles for specific workflows
  • Validation Rules: Add Python rules in roles/validate/files/rules/
  • Service Templates: Extend network service configurations

Developer Guide →

docs.title: Optimization Tips
docs.text:
1. Use Templates: Faster than OVA deployment when available
  2. Parallel Deployment: Configure Ansible forks in ansible.cfg
  3. Local OVAs: Faster than downloading from remote URLs
  4. Minimal Validation: Use targeted validation during development

docs.title: Resource Planning
docs.text: Environment Size Recommended Ansible Control Node Small (1-5 VMs) 2 CPU, 4 GB RAM Medium (6-20 VMs) 4 CPU, 8 GB RAM Large (21+ VMs) 8 CPU, 16 GB RAM

docs.title: Deployment Guide
docs.text: Master the deployment process for Testbed as Code.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The deploy role provisions VMs and configures services based on your validated configuration.

docs.title: Deployment Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Validated Config] --> B[VM Deployment]
    B --> C[Network Config]
    C --> D[Service Deploy]
    D --> E[App Deploy]
    E --> F[Verification]

    B1[Upload OVA] --> B2[Create VM]
    B2 --> B3[Power On]

    D1[DNS/DHCP/NTP] --> D2[TFTP/HTTP]
    D2 --> D3[TACACS+/LDAP/SNMP]

    B --> B1
    D --> D1

    style A fill:#049fd9,color:#fff
    style B fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#00c853,color:#fff
    style E fill:#00c853,color:#fff
    style F fill:#00c853,color:#fff

docs.title: Deploy from OVA
docs.text:
compute:
  vms:
    - name: "tbac_vm01"
      deploy_from: "ova"
      ova_image_name: "tbac-base-ubuntu"  # References images catalog
      # ... configuration ...

Process: 1. Upload OVA to vCenter (if not already present) 2. Deploy OVF from uploaded OVA 3. Customize VM settings (CPU, memory, network) 4. Apply cloud-init configuration 5. Power on VM

Time: 10-15 minutes per VM

docs.title: Deploy from Template (experimental)
docs.text:
compute:
  vms:
    - name: "tbac_vm01"
      deploy_from: "template"
      template:
        name: "ubuntu-24.04-template"
        guest_id: "ubuntu64Guest"
      # ... configuration ...

Process: 1. Clone from existing template 2. Customize VM settings 3. Apply cloud-init configuration 4. Power on VM

Time: 3-5 minutes per VM (faster than OVA)

docs.title: Full Deployment
docs.text:
Deploy everything:

ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml

docs.title: Partial Deployment
docs.text:
Deploy specific components using tags:

# Deploy VMs only
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy_vm

# Deploy base OS configuration (includes proxy)
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy_base

# Deploy proxy configuration only
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy_proxy

# Deploy network services only
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy_services

# Deploy LDAP service specifically
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy_ldap

docs.title: Available Tags
docs.text: Tag Scope merge Configuration merge only (common role) validate Full validation pipeline connectivity Infrastructure connectivity checks only ip_validation IP uniqueness checks only deploy Full deployment deploy_vm VM provisioning only deploy_base Base OS configuration (includes proxy) deploy_proxy Proxy configuration only deploy_services Network service deployment only deploy_ldap LDAP service specifically operate Full operations operate_ldap All LDAP operations (password reset + user sync + group sync) operate_ldap_sync_users LDAP user synchronization only operate_ldap_sync_groups LDAP group synchronization only operate_ldap_reset_password LDAP password reset for seed users only render_pyats pyATS testbed generation only

docs.title: Runtime Operations
docs.text:
After deployment, use the operate role for day-2 operations:

# Run all LDAP operations (password resets, user sync, group sync)
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags operate_ldap

docs.title: Real-time Progress
docs.text:
# Run with verbose output
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml -v

# Watch logs in another terminal
tail -f ansible.log

docs.text:
Check vCenter for deployment progress:

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Understanding the multi-tier validation system in Testbed as Code.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Testbed as Code uses a comprehensive validation system to ensure configuration correctness before deployment.

docs.title: Validation Layers
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Configuration] --> B[Schema Validation]
    B --> C{Valid?}
    C -->|No| D[Error Report]
    C -->|Yes| E[Semantic Validation]
    E --> F{Valid?}
    F -->|No| D
    F -->|Yes| G[Connectivity Tests]
    G --> H{Connected?}
    H -->|No| D
    H -->|Yes| I[IP Uniqueness Check]
    I --> J{IPs Free?}
    J -->|No| D
    J -->|Yes| K[Deployment Ready]

    style A fill:#049fd9,color:#fff
    style B fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#0a60ff,color:#fff

docs.title: Tier 1: Schema Validation
docs.text:
YAMALE-based structural validation:

# Run schema validation (standalone)
python scripts/validate_schema.py

# Or via Ansible
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags validate

Checks:

  • Required fields present
  • Correct data types
  • Enum values valid
  • IP address formats (ip(version=4))
  • List structures
  • Ansible Vault encrypted values (supported via custom !vault constructor)

docs.title: Tier 2: Semantic Validation
docs.text:
Python rules for business logic, executed by the testbed_semantic_validate action plugin with rules in roles/validate/files/rules/:

Rule Categories (executed in numeric order):

Category Rules What They Check testbed (1) 101 pyATS config validation Validates testbed.pyats.create_testbed_file is boolean and output_directory is non-empty string when present vms (4) 100 VM name uniqueness, 150 proxy config, 400 deployment conflicts, 700 IP address conflicts Unique VM names, proxy config validation, deploy method conflicts, IP duplicates within the data model hypervisors (5) 200 VM references, 201 vCenter config, 102 hostname protocol prefix, 500 infrastructure config, 600 host uniqueness VM-to-host references, vCenter required fields, protocol prefix stripping, infrastructure config, unique hosts network_services (16) 300 service references, 301 TFTP paths, 302 Docker image validation, 303 SNMP validation, 304 port conflict detection, 305 DHCP subnet overlap, 960 LDAP service required, 961 LDAP base DN format, 962 LDAP admin password, 970 TACACS+ service required, 971 TACACS+ host secret required, 972 TACACS+ user name uniqueness, 973 TACACS+ host name uniqueness, 974 TACACS+ profile name uniqueness, 975 TACACS+ spawnd ordering, 976 TACACS+ user member profile reference Service cross-references, TFTP paths, Docker image validation, SNMP credential strength and cisco-password restriction, SNMPv3 completeness, alert transport fields and transport references, port conflict detection across services, DHCP subnet overlap detection, LDAP service presence/base DN/admin password, TACACS+ service presence, host secrets, user/host/profile name uniqueness, spawnd min/max ordering, user profile membership images (2) 800 image artifact auth, 801 image remote source Conditional auth (artifactory requires url; auth optional but validated when present), remote source field completeness (protocol, ip, username, password, path_location required when remote is defined) application_services (1) 900 Netbox secret key length Validates that netbox.secret_key is at least 50 characters (Django minimum requirement)

docs.title: Tier 3: Connectivity Tests
docs.text:
Real infrastructure validation:

ansible-playbook tests/test_vcenter.yaml

Tests:

  • vCenter/ESXi accessibility
  • Authentication successful
  • Datastore exists and is accessible

docs.title: Tier 4: Network IP Availability
docs.text:
Ansible-native check (roles/validate/tasks/validate_ip_uniqueness.yaml) that probes the live network to ensure configured static IPs are not already in use before a deployment begins.

How it works:

  1. Queries all configured vCenter hosts (and standalone ESXi hosts) for existing VM names
  2. Automatically skips IP validation for any VM already present in vCenter/ESXi — safe for idempotent re-runs
  3. For new VMs, probes each configured static IP using a hybrid approach:
  4. ICMP ping — actively probes and populates ARP cache
  5. ARP table lookup — catches devices that block ICMP but still have a MAC entry
  6. Fails with a clear error listing every conflicting IP if any are already in use

Difference from rule 700_ip_address_conflicts

The 700_ip_address_conflicts semantic rule checks for IPs that appear more than once within your data model (e.g., two VMs configured with the same address). Tier 4 checks whether a configured IP is already responding on the live network — a separate and complementary concern.

ICMP-restricted environments

In environments where ICMP is blocked by firewalls or host-based policies, ping results are unreliable. The ARP check provides partial coverage for local subnets, but remote subnets require ICMP. Use skip_ip_validation in such environments (see below).

Skipping IP validation:

# Bypass Tier 4 for environments where ICMP/ARP detection is unreliable
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags validate -e "skip_ip_validation=true"

Running only IP validation:

ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags ip_validation

docs.title: Complete Validation
docs.text:
# All validation tiers (schema + semantic + connectivity)
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags validate

docs.title: Individual Validation
docs.text:
# Schema only (standalone script)
python scripts/validate_schema.py

# Schema only (with custom paths)
python scripts/validate_schema.py -s schema/schema.yaml -d data/testbed.tbac.yaml

# Connectivity only
ansible-playbook tests/test_vcenter.yaml

# IP uniqueness only
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags ip_validation

# Full validation, skipping IP uniqueness check
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags validate -e "skip_ip_validation=true"

docs.title: Schema Errors
docs.text:
Example: 

Schema validation failed:
  testbed.hypervisor.vcenter[0].datacenters: Required field missing

Fix: Add the required datacenters list to your vCenter configuration.

docs.title: Semantic Errors
docs.text:
Example: 

Semantic validation failed:
  Image 'tbac-base-ubuntu' referenced by VM 'tbac_vm01' not found in catalog

Fix: Add the image to your images catalog or fix the image name reference.

docs.title: Connectivity Errors
docs.text:
Example: 

Failed to connect to vCenter 'vcenter.lab.local': Connection refused

Fix: Verify network connectivity and vCenter is running.

docs.title: IP Conflict Errors
docs.text:
Example: 

IP address conflict: 10.196.209.120, 10.196.209.121 already in use.
Please choose different IP addresses in your testbed configuration.

Fix: The listed addresses are responding on the network. Either:

  • Choose different static IPs in your testbed.tbac.yaml
  • If the IPs belong to VMs that are already deployed (e.g., from a previous run), verify that those VMs are registered in vCenter/ESXi so the check can automatically exclude them
  • If the environment blocks ICMP/ARP and the detection is a false positive, use skip_ip_validation=true

docs.title: Validation Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Validate early and often during configuration changes
  2. Review validation output carefully before deployment
  3. Fix errors incrementally starting with schema errors
  4. Test connectivity before committing configuration

docs.text: Understanding the complete Testbed as Code workflow from configuration to deployment.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Testbed as Code follows a structured workflow that ensures safe, validated deployments.

docs.title: Phase 1: Configuration (common role)
docs.text:
The common role merges all configuration files:

ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags merge

Steps: 1. Merge all files from defaults/ directory 2. Merge all files from data/ directory 3. Combine defaults + data with intelligent merge logic 4. Generate .tbac/current.tbac.yaml

Learn more about Merge System →

docs.title: Phase 2: Validation (validate role)
docs.text:
Configuration passes through the 4-tier validation pipeline (schema, semantic, connectivity, and IP uniqueness checks) before deployment proceeds:

ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags validate

Learn more about Validation →

docs.title: Phase 3: Deployment (deploy role)
docs.text:
Provisions infrastructure based on validated configuration:

ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy

Deployment Steps: 1. Upload OVA files (if needed) 2. Deploy VMs from templates or OVA 3. Configure network interfaces 4. Apply cloud-init customization 5. Deploy network services (DHCP, DNS, NTP, TFTP, HTTP, LDAP, TACACS+, SNMP/LibreNMS) 6. Configure application services

Learn more about Deployment →

docs.title: Phase 4: Verification
docs.text:
Verify successful deployment:

# Check generated pyATS testbed
cat out/pyats_testbed.yaml

# Verify VM accessibility
ssh user@vm-ip-address

# Review change tracking
cat .tbac/run_map.yaml

docs.title: Complete Workflow
docs.text:
Run all phases in sequence:

ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml

docs.title: Selective Execution
docs.text:
Run specific phases using tags:

# Merge only
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags merge

# Validate only (includes merge)
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags validate

# Deploy only (includes merge and validate)
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml --tags deploy

docs.title: Multi-Environment Workflow
docs.text:
Deploy to different environments:

# Development
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml -e "data_dir=data-dev"

# Staging
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml -e "data_dir=data-staging"

# Production
ansible-playbook playbooks/tbac.yaml -e "data_dir=data-prod"

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Always validate first: Run validation before deployment
  2. Review changes: Check .tbac/changes.diff before proceeding
  3. Test in dev: Use non-production environment first
  4. Commit configs: Version control all configuration changes
  5. Document changes: Use descriptive commit messages
